female-32-uncomplicated.name: Katherine Mitchell
female-32-uncomplicated.chiefComplaint: It burns when I pee and I feel like I have to go all the time

missedConsequences.Uncomplicated Cystitis: Untreated cystitis causes prolonged symptoms and can ascend to pyelonephritis.
missedConsequences.Pyelonephritis: Missed upper tract infection leads to sepsis, renal abscess, and permanent kidney damage.
missedConsequences.STI (Chlamydia/Gonorrhea): Missed STI leads to PID, infertility, and ongoing transmission to partners.

commonPitfalls: Treating empirically without considering STI in sexually active patients
Using fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis (reserved for complicated UTI/pyelonephritis)
Not recognizing risk factors for complicated UTI (male, pregnancy, diabetes, immunocompromise)
Missing pyelonephritis by not asking about flank pain and fever
Obtaining unnecessary urine culture for uncomplicated cystitis in young women

mcqs.question: For uncomplicated cystitis in a non-pregnant woman, which antibiotic regimen is FIRST-LINE per IDSA guidelines?

options.explanation: Fluoroquinolones are NOT first-line for uncomplicated cystitis due to resistance and side effects. Reserved for complicated UTI/pyelonephritis.

options.text: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x 5 days OR TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days
options.explanation: IDSA first-line: Nitrofurantoin (Macrobid) x5 days, TMP-SMX x3 days (if local resistance <20%), or Fosfomycin single dose.
Amoxicillin has high resistance rates for UTI pathogens and is not recommended.
Parenteral therapy not needed for uncomplicated cystitis. This would be for gonorrhea or complicated UTI.

allDiagnoses.name: Uncomplicated Cystitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic triad of dysuria, frequency, urgency in young healthy woman without fever or flank pain.

allDiagnoses.name: Urethritis (STI-related)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sexually active, new partner. Chlamydia/gonorrhea can cause similar symptoms. Should be considered.

allDiagnoses.name: Pyelonephritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must rule out with absence of fever, flank pain, systemic symptoms. Ascending infection is serious.

allDiagnoses.name: Chlamydia/Gonorrhea
allDiagnoses.rationale: New sexual partner warrants STI testing. Can cause urethritis mimicking UTI.

allDiagnoses.name: Complicated UTI
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must consider if any complicating factors present (pregnancy, diabetes, immunocompromise, anatomic abnormality).

allDiagnoses.name: Vaginitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause dysuria but typically has discharge and vaginal symptoms. Ask about discharge.

allDiagnoses.name: Interstitial Cystitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Chronic condition with similar symptoms but no infection. Would have recurrent negative cultures.

allDiagnoses.name: Kidney Stone
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause dysuria but usually with colicky flank pain and hematuria. Less likely presentation.

female-32-uncomplicated.oldcartsNarrative: "It started about 2 days ago. Every time I pee it burns really bad, especially toward the end. I feel like I have to go all the time even when nothing comes out. I'm running to the bathroom every 30 minutes. The burning is probably a 6 out of 10. My urine looks a little cloudy and maybe smells stronger than usual. The urge to pee is really intense - I feel like I can't hold it. Then only a little comes out and it hurts the whole time."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: It started about 2 days ago. The burning started first, then I noticed I was going to the bathroom more and more frequently.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute onset over days is typical for UTI. Chronic symptoms might suggest other diagnoses.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the burning - is it during urination or after?
historyQuestions.answer: It burns the whole time I'm peeing, especially toward the end. Then it stops after I'm done.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Terminal dysuria is classic for cystitis (bladder irritation)

historyQuestions.text: How often are you urinating?
historyQuestions.answer: Every 30 minutes or so, and I feel like I HAVE to go right away or I'll have an accident. But then only a little comes out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Frequency and urgency are cardinal UTI symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any urine leakage or accidents?
historyQuestions.answer: Almost! The urge is so intense that I've almost leaked a few times trying to get to the bathroom in time. No actual accidents though, thankfully.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Urge incontinence can occur with UTI due to bladder irritation and detrusor overactivity. Important to ask as it affects quality of life and confirms urgency severity.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any fevers or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: No fever. I've checked a couple times and it's been normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever would suggest pyelonephritis, not just cystitis

historyQuestions.text: Any pain in your back or sides?
historyQuestions.answer: No, no back pain at all. Just the bladder area.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Flank pain would suggest upper tract infection (pyelonephritis)

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any blood in your urine?
historyQuestions.answer: I thought I saw a little pink tinge yesterday, but not a lot.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Microscopic or gross hematuria is common with cystitis

historyQuestions.text: Any vaginal discharge or itching?
historyQuestions.answer: No discharge or itching. That all seems normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Discharge would suggest vaginitis or STI rather than UTI

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your sexual history
historyQuestions.answer: I've been with a new boyfriend for about 2 months. We use condoms most of the time but not always.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New partner and inconsistent condom use warrants STI consideration

historyQuestions.text: Are you sexually active?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've been with a new boyfriend for about 2 months.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sexual activity relevant for STI risk and post-coital UTI

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had a sexually transmitted infection?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've never had an STI. I got tested when I started seeing my current boyfriend and everything was negative.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior STI history increases risk of current infection

historyQuestions.text: Do you use protection during sex?
historyQuestions.answer: We use condoms sometimes, but not every time. I'm on the pill so I'm not worried about pregnancy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Inconsistent condom use increases STI risk

historyQuestions.text: Do you use any recreational drugs?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't use any drugs. I've never really been into that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: IV drug use would be relevant for certain infections

historyQuestions.text: Any history of alcohol or drug use?
historyQuestions.answer: I drink socially - maybe a couple drinks on weekends with friends. No drugs though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Social history for completeness

historyQuestions.text: Do you notice symptoms after intercourse?
historyQuestions.answer: Now that you mention it, the symptoms did start a day after we were together. That happened with my UTI in college too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Post-coital UTIs are common due to urethral trauma and bacterial introduction - important for prevention counseling

historyQuestions.text: Do you urinate after sex?
historyQuestions.answer: Not usually, I usually just fall asleep. Should I be doing that?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Post-coital voiding is recommended for UTI prevention - opportunity for counseling

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: Just finished last week. It was normal - 5 days, regular flow.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent normal period helps rule out pregnancy; timing confirms contraception efficacy

historyQuestions.text: Any chance you could be pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I just had my period last week and I'm on the pill. I take it every day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pregnancy changes UTI from uncomplicated to complicated - changes management

historyQuestions.text: What kind of birth control do you use?
historyQuestions.answer: Oral contraceptive pills. I've been on them for about 5 years. We also use condoms sometimes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Some contraceptive methods (spermicides, diaphragms) increase UTI risk

historyQuestions.text: Have you had UTIs before?
historyQuestions.answer: Once in college, maybe 10 years ago. It felt just like this and antibiotics cleared it up quickly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: History helps with diagnosis; recurrent UTIs might need different workup

historyQuestions.text: Any medical conditions like diabetes?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. Just take birth control.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diabetes would make this complicated UTI

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: Sulfa drugs - I got a bad rash once. No other allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before prescribing - sulfa allergy rules out TMP-SMX

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just my birth control pill - I've been on it for years. Sometimes ibuprofen for headaches. That's it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication list important for interactions and identifies immunosuppression

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm pretty healthy. I get migraines sometimes but nothing major. Never been hospitalized except when I was born.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms uncomplicated status - no diabetes, immunocompromise, or structural abnormalities

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries ever. I've been lucky.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior urologic or pelvic surgery could predispose to complicated UTI

historyQuestions.text: Any medical problems that run in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: My mom has thyroid issues and my dad has high blood pressure. Nothing with kidneys or bladders.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of kidney disease or recurrent UTIs might indicate predisposition

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke or drink alcohol?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't smoke. I drink socially - maybe a couple drinks on weekends with friends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Social history for completeness

historyQuestions.text: When did you last see your regular doctor?
historyQuestions.answer: About 6 months ago for my annual exam. Pap smear and everything was normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent GYN exam and baseline health status

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a marketing coordinator at an advertising agency. Office work mostly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Work environment and bathroom access can affect UTI risk

historyQuestions.text: How much water do you usually drink?
historyQuestions.answer: Probably not enough honestly. Maybe 3-4 glasses a day. I drink a lot of coffee at work.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fluid intake affects UTI risk and is important for prevention counseling

historyQuestions.text: What are you hoping we can do for you today?
historyQuestions.answer: I just want antibiotics to make this go away. It's miserable - I can't concentrate at work because I'm always running to the bathroom.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Understanding expectations helps with patient-centered care

physicalExam.reasoning: Afebrile with normal vitals - reassuring against pyelonephritis/sepsis

physicalExam.finding: Well-appearing young woman in no acute distress. Comfortable.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-toxic appearance supports uncomplicated infection

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. Mild suprapubic tenderness to deep palpation. No rebound or guarding.
physicalExam.reasoning: Suprapubic tenderness is classic for cystitis

physicalExam.finding: No CVA tenderness bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: CVA tenderness would indicate pyelonephritis - its absence supports cystitis only

labs.name: Urinalysis (dipstick and microscopy)
labs.result: Nitrite positive, Leukocyte esterase 3+, WBC >50/hpf, bacteria present, few RBCs. No casts.
labs.reasoning: Supports UTI diagnosis. Nitrite indicates bacteria converting nitrate. LE indicates pyuria.

labs.name: Urine Culture
labs.result: Not routinely indicated for uncomplicated cystitis. Would obtain for recurrent UTI or treatment failure.
labs.reasoning: IDSA: Culture optional for uncomplicated cystitis. Empiric treatment appropriate.

labs.name: Chlamydia/Gonorrhea NAAT
labs.result: Obtained given new partner. Results pending.
labs.reasoning: New sexual partner with inconsistent protection warrants STI screening

labs.name: Urine Pregnancy Test
labs.reasoning: Confirms uncomplicated status. Pregnancy would change antibiotic choice.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated
imaging.result: Imaging not needed for uncomplicated cystitis
imaging.reasoning: Reserve imaging for complicated UTI, pyelonephritis, or suspected structural abnormality

treatments.name: Nitrofurantoin (Macrobid)
treatments.result: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x 5 days
treatments.reasoning: IDSA first-line for uncomplicated cystitis. Concentrates in urine, minimal resistance, narrow spectrum.

treatments.name: TMP-SMX (Bactrim DS)
treatments.result: TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days (if local E.coli resistance <20%)
treatments.reasoning: First-line alternative if local resistance rates acceptable. Shorter course. Note: patient has sulfa allergy - contraindicated in this case.

treatments.name: Fosfomycin (Monurol)
treatments.result: Fosfomycin 3g single dose
treatments.reasoning: Single dose option, good for adherence. Slightly less effective than multi-day regimens.

treatments.name: Ciprofloxacin/Levofloxacin
treatments.result: NOT first-line for uncomplicated cystitis
treatments.reasoning: Fluoroquinolones reserved for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI due to resistance and adverse effects (tendinopathy, neuropathy)

treatments.name: Phenazopyridine (Pyridium)
treatments.result: 200mg TID x 2 days for symptomatic relief
treatments.reasoning: Bladder analgesic for symptom relief. Warn about orange urine. Don't use >2 days.

treatments.name: Increased fluid intake
treatments.result: Encourage plenty of fluids
treatments.reasoning: Helps flush bacteria. General supportive care.

disposition.name: Discharge with oral antibiotics
disposition.result: Appropriate for uncomplicated cystitis
disposition.reasoning: Uncomplicated UTI is outpatient diagnosis. Return if fever, flank pain, or worsening symptoms.

disposition.name: Hospital admission
disposition.result: Not indicated for uncomplicated cystitis
disposition.reasoning: Admission only for complicated UTI, pyelonephritis with sepsis, or inability to tolerate PO

actualDiagnosis.name: Uncomplicated Cystitis (Acute Bacterial Cystitis)
keyFindings: No flank pain or CVA tenderness (rules out pyelonephritis)
Urinalysis: positive nitrites, leukocyte esterase, pyuria, bacteriuria
teachingPoints: Uncomplicated UTI: Healthy, non-pregnant woman with cystitis. No structural/functional abnormality.
Complicated UTI: Male, pregnancy, diabetes, immunocompromise, structural abnormality, catheter, recent instrumentation, antibiotic-resistant organism
IDSA first-line for uncomplicated cystitis: Nitrofurantoin x5d, TMP-SMX x3d (if resistance <20%), Fosfomycin single dose
Do NOT use fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis - reserve for complicated UTI/pyelonephritis
Urine culture NOT required for uncomplicated cystitis in young women - empiric treatment appropriate
Pyelonephritis features: Fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, nausea/vomiting, systemic illness
Always consider STI (chlamydia/gonorrhea) in sexually active patients with urinary symptoms
Recurrent UTI (≥3/year): Consider post-coital prophylaxis, self-start therapy, or daily suppression

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Guidelines for Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis 2011
guidelineReferences.citation: Gupta K et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(5):e103-e120
keyPoints: First-line: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x 5 days
Alternative: TMP-SMX 160/800 BID x 3 days (if local resistance <20%)
Alternative: Fosfomycin 3g single dose
Avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis
Urine culture not required for typical uncomplicated presentation

guidelineReferences.name: AUA/CUA/SUFU Guideline on Recurrent UTI 2019
guidelineReferences.citation: American Urological Association
keyPoints: Recurrent UTI: ≥2 infections in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months
Consider prophylaxis options: post-coital, self-start, continuous
Cranberry products may reduce recurrence (moderate evidence)
Evaluate for structural abnormalities if atypical presentation
Vaginal estrogen may reduce recurrence in postmenopausal women

whatIfScenarios.title: What if she had fever and flank pain?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same symptoms but now with 101.8°F fever and left flank pain

chiefComplaint.original: It burns when I pee and I feel like I have to go all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: It burns when I pee, I feel like I have to go all the time, and now I have fever and my left side hurts

historyAnswers.fever: Yes, I've had fever since yesterday. I checked it this morning and it was 101.8°F. I've also had chills.
historyAnswers.chills: Yes, I've been having chills and I felt like I had the flu.
historyAnswers.back_pain: Yes, my left side really hurts - in my back, around where my kidney would be. It's a dull aching pain.
historyAnswers.flank_pain: Yes, my left flank has been hurting since yesterday. It's tender when I touch it.
historyAnswers.nausea: I've been feeling nauseous and I threw up once this morning.
historyAnswers.systemic: I feel really sick overall - weak, tired, and just unwell.

examFindings.cva_tenderness: Significant left CVA tenderness on percussion. Patient winces and guards.
examFindings.abdominal_exam: Mild left flank tenderness. No peritoneal signs. Suprapubic tenderness present.

feedback.correct: Excellent! Fever + flank pain = pyelonephritis until proven otherwise. This is a complicated infection requiring different antibiotics and possibly imaging.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of upper tract involvement. How does this change antibiotic choice and duration?
feedback.incorrect: Fever and flank pain indicate pyelonephritis - this is NOT uncomplicated cystitis. Requires broader antibiotics, longer duration, and possible imaging.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Cystitis = lower tract, no systemic symptoms, 3-5 day treatment. Pyelonephritis = upper tract, fever/flank pain, 7-14 day treatment with fluoroquinolone or IV antibiotics. Need urine culture, consider blood cultures, and CT if no improvement in 48-72h.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was male?
whatIfScenarios.description: A 55-year-old man presents with the same urinary symptoms

chiefComplaint.original: It burns when I pee and I feel like I have to go all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: It burns when I pee and I feel like I have to go all the time. I'm also having trouble starting my stream.

historyAnswers.sexual_history: I've been married for 25 years. My wife is my only partner.
historyAnswers.sexually_active: Yes, but just with my wife. We've been together 25 years.
historyAnswers.discharge: I haven't noticed any discharge from my penis.
historyAnswers.voiding: I've been having trouble starting my stream for the past year or so. I get up 2-3 times a night to pee. Sometimes I feel like I can't empty my bladder completely.

examFindings.dre: Prostate is enlarged, approximately 40g, firm but smooth. Mildly tender to palpation. No nodules.
examFindings.genital_exam: Normal male genitalia. No testicular tenderness or swelling. No urethral discharge.

feedback.correct: Excellent! UTI in a male is ALWAYS considered complicated. Must evaluate for prostatitis, structural abnormality, and obtain urine culture.
feedback.partial: Good recognition that male UTI differs. What additional evaluation is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Male UTI is NEVER 'uncomplicated.' Always evaluate for prostatitis (tender prostate on DRE), consider structural causes, and treat longer (7-14 days minimum).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Female uncomplicated cystitis: short course, no culture needed. Male UTI: always complicated, always culture, evaluate for prostatitis/BPH, longer treatment (2-4 weeks if prostatitis), may need prostate imaging.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was pregnant?
whatIfScenarios.description: The patient is 20 weeks pregnant with the same symptoms

chiefComplaint.original: It burns when I pee and I feel like I have to go all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: It burns when I pee and I feel like I have to go all the time. I'm 20 weeks pregnant.

historyAnswers.pregnancy: Yes, I'm 20 weeks pregnant. This is my first pregnancy and everything has been going well until now.
historyAnswers.lmp: My last period was about 20 weeks ago. I'm due in about 4.5 months.
historyAnswers.prenatal_care: I've been getting regular prenatal care. Everything was normal at my last visit 2 weeks ago.
historyAnswers.contractions: No contractions that I've noticed. No abdominal cramping.

examFindings.abdomen: Gravid uterus consistent with 20 weeks. Non-tender. No contractions palpated.
examFindings.fetal_heart: Fetal heart tones present at 150 bpm by doppler.

feedback.correct: Excellent! UTI in pregnancy is ALWAYS complicated. Higher risk of pyelonephritis, requires culture, and certain antibiotics contraindicated (fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX in 1st tri).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of pregnancy as complicating factor. What antibiotics are safe?
feedback.incorrect: Pregnancy makes any UTI complicated. Even asymptomatic bacteriuria requires treatment in pregnancy due to pyelonephritis/preterm labor risk.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Pregnancy UTI: always culture, always treat (even asymptomatic bacteriuria). Safe antibiotics: nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalosporins, amoxicillin-clavulanate. Avoid: fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX (1st trimester).

guidelineReferences.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
keyPoints: Sepsis and septic shock are medical emergencies - treatment and resuscitation begin immediately (Best Practice)
For septic shock or high likelihood of sepsis: antimicrobials within 1 hour of recognition (Strong)
For possible sepsis without shock: antimicrobials within 3 hours if concern persists (Weak)
30 mL/kg IV crystalloid within first 3 hours for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion (Weak)
Target MAP ≥65 mmHg; norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor (Strong)
Use balanced crystalloids over normal saline for resuscitation (Weak)
Recommend against qSOFA alone as screening tool - use SIRS, NEWS, or MEWS (Strong)
Guide resuscitation using dynamic measures, lactate trends, and capillary refill time
Consider IV corticosteroids for septic shock requiring ongoing vasopressors (Weak)
ICU admission within 6 hours for patients requiring ICU-level care (Weak)

female-78-uti-sepsis.name: Emily Martinez
female-78-uti-sepsis.chiefComplaint: My mother is confused and not acting right
female-78-uti-sepsis.variantDescription: Sepsis secondary to urinary tract infection (urosepsis)

missedConsequences.Sepsis: Untreated sepsis progresses to septic shock and multiorgan failure with mortality 20-40%.
missedConsequences.Urinary Tract Infection/Pyelonephritis: Source must be identified and treated. Delay increases mortality.
missedConsequences.Meningitis: Confusion and fever could represent CNS infection - catastrophic if missed.

commonPitfalls: Delaying antibiotics - each hour delay increases mortality 7-8%
Inadequate fluid resuscitation (need 30 mL/kg crystalloid)
Not obtaining cultures BEFORE antibiotics (but don't delay abx for cultures)
Missing the source - always look for focus of infection
Not recognizing sepsis in elderly (may be hypothermic, not febrile)
Using inadequate antibiotic coverage initially

mcqs.question: The patient has fever, confusion, tachycardia, hypotension, and suspected UTI. Blood cultures are pending. What is the MOST important next step?

options.text: Wait for culture results to guide antibiotic therapy
options.explanation: NEVER delay antibiotics for cultures in sepsis. Get cultures, then immediately give empiric antibiotics.

options.text: IV fluid bolus and empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately
options.explanation: Sepsis requires immediate intervention: IV crystalloid (30 mL/kg) and broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour. Each hour delay increases mortality.

options.text: CT head to evaluate altered mental status
options.explanation: Encephalopathy is common in sepsis and doesn't require CT unless focal neuro signs or concern for primary CNS process.

options.text: Arterial line placement for blood pressure monitoring
options.explanation: Monitoring is important but resuscitation and antibiotics are the priority. Don't delay treatment for procedures.

mcqs.question: After 2L of crystalloid, BP is still 85/50 and lactate is 4.2. What is the NEXT best step?

options.text: Continue IV fluids up to 6-8 liters
options.explanation: After initial 30 mL/kg, if still hypotensive, need vasopressors. Excessive fluids cause harm.
Septic shock = sepsis + hypotension refractory to fluids OR lactate >2. Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor. Target MAP ≥65.
Double antibiotic coverage isn't the issue. Hemodynamic support with vasopressors is needed.

options.text: Emergency dialysis for presumed acute kidney injury
options.explanation: AKI in sepsis is usually from hypoperfusion. Treating the sepsis and maintaining perfusion is priority.

allDiagnoses.name: Sepsis (Urosepsis)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fever, altered mental status, tachycardia, hypotension with UTI symptoms. Meets sepsis criteria.

allDiagnoses.name: Urinary Tract Infection/Pyelonephritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Urinary symptoms, pyuria, bacteriuria - source of sepsis.

allDiagnoses.name: Meningitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fever and altered mental status could be meningitis. Assess for nuchal rigidity, consider LP if indicated.

allDiagnoses.name: Septic Shock
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hypotension despite fluids + elevated lactate = septic shock. Higher mortality requires aggressive management.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common source of sepsis. Assess lungs, get chest X-ray.

allDiagnoses.name: Intra-abdominal Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cholecystitis, appendicitis, diverticulitis can cause sepsis. Examine abdomen carefully.

allDiagnoses.name: Delirium from other cause
allDiagnoses.rationale: Infection is clearly present. Other causes possible but treat infection first.

allDiagnoses.name: Drug reaction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Possible but fever + hypotension + infection signs point to sepsis.

female-78-uti-sepsis.oldcartsNarrative: (History from daughter): "My mother has been confused since yesterday. She doesn't know where she is and isn't making sense. At first she was just a bit 'off,' but by evening she didn't recognize me and was saying things that didn't make sense. She seems weak and hasn't been eating or drinking much. She was fine last week - this came on suddenly. I've never seen her like this before - she's usually sharp as a tack."

historyQuestions.text: When did this all start?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She was completely normal last week. About 3 days ago she mentioned burning when she urinated. Yesterday morning she started acting confused, and it's gotten worse since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline crucial - acute onset helps identify infection as cause rather than chronic dementia

historyQuestions.text: How long has she been confused?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Since yesterday morning. At first she was just a bit 'off,' but by evening she didn't recognize me and was saying things that didn't make sense.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute altered mental status in setting of infection = sepsis-associated encephalopathy. Common in elderly.

historyQuestions.text: When did the fever start?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She felt warm yesterday. I took her temperature this morning and it was 102.8°F.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever + organ dysfunction (confusion) = sepsis

historyQuestions.text: Any urinary symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She mentioned burning with urination and going to the bathroom a lot 2 days ago, but she's been too confused to tell me more now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Preceding dysuria and frequency suggest UTI as source of sepsis

historyQuestions.text: What is her baseline mental status?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She's usually sharp as a tack. Lives alone, pays her bills, watches her game shows. This is not her.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishing baseline is crucial - this confusion is acute and represents organ dysfunction

historyQuestions.text: Has she been eating and drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Barely anything for 2 days. Just a few sips of water. She's been too weak.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Poor intake compounds volume depletion in sepsis

historyQuestions.text: What medical problems does she have?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Diabetes and high blood pressure. She takes metformin and lisinopril.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diabetes increases infection risk and can mask symptoms. ACEi may contribute to hypotension.

historyQuestions.text: Any cough or breathing problems?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) No cough. Her breathing seems faster than normal but no cough.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing for pneumonia as alternative source. Tachypnea is from sepsis, not primary lung disease here.

historyQuestions.text: Any abdominal pain or nausea?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She hasn't complained of belly pain. Some nausea but no vomiting.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing for abdominal source (cholecystitis, etc.)

historyQuestions.text: Does she have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) No, no allergies that we know of. She's taken antibiotics before without problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before broad-spectrum antibiotics - no allergies allows full antibiotic options

historyQuestions.text: Can you tell me all of her medications?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Metformin for diabetes, lisinopril for blood pressure, and she takes a baby aspirin every day. Oh, and calcium with vitamin D for her bones.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Full medication list important - metformin needs to be held, aspirin may affect bleeding risk

historyQuestions.text: Has she had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She had a hysterectomy years ago, maybe in her 50s. And cataract surgery on both eyes. Nothing recent.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history for anesthesia risk if needed and to identify potential infection sources

historyQuestions.text: Any medical problems that run in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Diabetes runs in the family - my grandmother had it too. Heart disease - my dad had a heart attack. Nothing infectious though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history helps establish baseline risk factors

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about her living situation
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She lives alone in her own house but I check on her every day. She's usually very independent - cooks for herself, drives to church. This is not like her at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Baseline functional status critical - this acute change from baseline confirms serious illness

historyQuestions.text: Has she been to the doctor recently or been hospitalized?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She saw her doctor about 2 months ago for a regular checkup. No hospitalizations in years. She's been very healthy until now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent hospitalization would change pathogen considerations; baseline health reassuring

historyQuestions.text: Has your mother talked about what she would want if she got very sick?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She's always said she wants everything done. We talked about it after my dad passed. She has a living will at home that says full code.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Code status and goals of care essential in elderly patient with serious illness

historyQuestions.text: Is there a healthcare proxy or someone who makes decisions if she can't?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Yes, I'm her healthcare proxy. We have all the paperwork done.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Healthcare proxy important as patient currently lacks capacity due to delirium

historyQuestions.text: Is she up to date on her vaccines?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She got her flu shot in the fall. I think she had the pneumonia shot a few years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vaccination status affects infection risk and pathogen considerations

physicalExam.reasoning: SIRS criteria met: Temp >38°C, HR >90, RR >20. Hypotension indicates possible septic shock.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly female appearing acutely ill. Confused, not following commands consistently. Picking at sheets. Dry mucous membranes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Acutely ill with encephalopathy - organ dysfunction criterion for sepsis

physicalExam.finding: Alert but disoriented to time, place, person. Inattentive - unable to count backwards. No focal motor deficits. No nuchal rigidity. Moves all extremities.
physicalExam.reasoning: Encephalopathy without focal signs or meningismus. Sepsis-associated delirium. Absence of nuchal rigidity reassuring but doesn't rule out meningitis in elderly.

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs. JVP flat. Capillary refill 4 seconds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia compensating for vasodilation. Flat JVP and delayed cap refill indicate poor perfusion.

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Tachypneic but no crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clear lungs make pneumonia less likely as source. Tachypnea from compensating for acidosis.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness. No rebound or guarding. No RUQ tenderness. Bowel sounds present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Suprapubic tenderness suggests bladder involvement (cystitis progressed to sepsis)

physicalExam.finding: Mild tenderness to percussion over left CVA.
physicalExam.reasoning: CVA tenderness suggests upper tract involvement (pyelonephritis) - more serious than cystitis

physicalExam.finding: Warm, flushed skin. No rashes. No petechiae. Mottling of knees bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Warm skin from vasodilation (early sepsis). Mottling indicates hypoperfusion - concerning sign.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 18.2 (left shift with 15% bands), Hgb 11.8, Plt 145
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with bandemia indicates bacterial infection. Thrombocytopenia can occur in sepsis.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 138, K 4.8, Cl 102, CO2 18, BUN 42, Cr 2.1 (baseline 0.9), Glucose 245
labs.reasoning: Acute kidney injury from sepsis (Cr 2.1 from 0.9). Low CO2 = metabolic acidosis. Stress hyperglycemia.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion. Lactate >2 with hypotension = septic shock.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Cloudy, large leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, >50 WBC/hpf, bacteria present, few RBCs
labs.reasoning: Strongly positive UA confirms UTI as source. Pyuria, nitrites, bacteriuria diagnostic.

labs.name: Blood Cultures (x2 sets)
labs.result: Drawn before antibiotics. Results pending (typically 24-48 hours).
labs.reasoning: Essential for identifying organism and sensitivities. Draw before antibiotics but don't delay treatment.

labs.name: Urine Culture
labs.reasoning: Will identify organism. E. coli most common in urosepsis.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Elevated procalcitonin supports bacterial infection. Can help guide duration of antibiotics.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.reasoning: Mild elevations can occur in sepsis (hypoperfusion hepatitis). No evidence of biliary source.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No infiltrate, consolidation, or effusion. Heart size normal.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out pneumonia as source. Important to check even with clear lung exam.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: Consider if concern for complicated UTI (abscess), obstructing stone, or alternative source
imaging.reasoning: Not routinely needed for uncomplicated urosepsis. Order if not responding to treatment or concern for abscess.

treatments.name: IV Fluid Resuscitation
treatments.result: 30 mL/kg crystalloid (approximately 2L for this patient) within first 3 hours
treatments.reasoning: Surviving Sepsis guideline: 30 mL/kg crystalloid for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion. Usually LR or NS.

treatments.name: Empiric Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics
treatments.result: Ceftriaxone 2g IV (or piperacillin-tazobactam if concern for resistant organisms). Give within 1 hour.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition. Each hour delay increases mortality 7-8%.

treatments.name: Vasopressor if fluid-refractory
treatments.result: Norepinephrine started at 0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg
treatments.reasoning: If hypotensive after 30 mL/kg fluids, start norepinephrine. First-line vasopressor in septic shock.

treatments.name: Repeat Lactate
treatments.result: Recheck lactate in 2-4 hours to assess response to treatment
treatments.reasoning: Lactate clearance is important marker of resuscitation adequacy. Target >10% clearance.

treatments.name: Foley Catheter
treatments.result: Insert Foley to monitor urine output. Target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr.
treatments.reasoning: Urine output is key marker of perfusion. Necessary for monitoring in sepsis.

treatments.name: Hold metformin
treatments.result: Hold metformin given AKI and risk of lactic acidosis
treatments.reasoning: Metformin contraindicated in AKI and can worsen lactic acidosis

consults.name: ICU team/Rapid Response
consults.result: ICU admission for septic shock with vasopressor requirement
consults.reasoning: Septic shock requiring vasopressors needs ICU-level care

disposition.name: ICU Admission
disposition.result: Admit to ICU for septic shock management
disposition.reasoning: Septic shock with vasopressor requirement, AKI, and encephalopathy needs ICU.

actualDiagnosis.name: Septic Shock Secondary to Urosepsis (Pyelonephritis)
keyFindings: Infection: UTI symptoms (dysuria, frequency) progressed to sepsis
SIRS criteria: Temp 102.8°F, HR 118, RR 24, WBC 18.2 with bandemia
Organ dysfunction: Acute encephalopathy (new confusion), AKI (Cr 2.1 from 0.9)
Source identified: Positive UA with pyuria, bacteriuria, nitrites. CVA tenderness.
Signs of hypoperfusion: Mottled skin, delayed cap refill, flat JVP, oliguria
teachingPoints: Sepsis = suspected infection + organ dysfunction (use qSOFA or SOFA score)
qSOFA (quick SOFA): RR ≥22, Altered mental status, SBP ≤100. ≥2 suggests sepsis outside ICU.
Septic SHOCK = Sepsis + (hypotension requiring vasopressors OR lactate >2 despite fluids)
Hour-1 Bundle: Measure lactate, obtain cultures, administer antibiotics, start fluids, vasopressors if needed
Antibiotics within 1 hour - each hour delay increases mortality 7-8%
30 mL/kg crystalloid is initial resuscitation target, then reassess
Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor for septic shock, target MAP ≥65
Most common sources: Lung (pneumonia), Urinary tract, Abdomen, Skin/soft tissue, Line infections
Elderly may present atypically: hypothermia instead of fever, confusion as main symptom
Lactate clearance (>10% in 2-4 hours) is marker of adequate resuscitation

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Each hour delay in antibiotics increases septic shock mortality by 7.6%
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Kumar A et al., Crit Care Med 2006
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Antibiotics within 1 hour is critical - don't delay for cultures, imaging, or procedures
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Survival decreased from 79.9% (first hour) to 42% (by 6 hours) with antibiotic delay

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Protocol-based resuscitation did NOT improve outcomes vs usual care
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Early recognition and treatment matters more than specific protocols. CVP/ScvO2 targets no longer mandatory.
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 60-day mortality: 21.0% (protocol) vs 18.9% (usual care) - no significant difference

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Balanced crystalloids reduced death, new RRT, or persistent renal dysfunction vs saline
evidenceBasedPearls.source: SMART Trial, Semler MW et al., NEJM 2018
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use lactated Ringer's or Plasma-Lyte over normal saline for sepsis resuscitation
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Major adverse kidney events: 14.3% (balanced) vs 15.4% (saline); NNT 94

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Adding vasopressin to norepinephrine may reduce mortality in less severe shock
evidenceBasedPearls.source: VASST Trial, Russell JA et al., NEJM 2008
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Consider vasopressin 0.03 U/min as second-line agent to reduce norepinephrine dose
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 28-day mortality in less severe shock: 26.5% (vasopressin) vs 35.7% (norepinephrine alone)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Hydrocortisone did not reduce 90-day mortality but accelerated shock reversal
evidenceBasedPearls.source: ADRENAL Trial, Venkatesh B et al., NEJM 2018
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use hydrocortisone 200mg/day for refractory shock requiring escalating vasopressors
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 90-day mortality: 27.9% vs 28.8% (NS); Faster shock resolution (median 3 vs 4 days)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Peripheral perfusion-guided resuscitation non-inferior to lactate-guided
evidenceBasedPearls.source: ANDROMEDA-SHOCK, Hernández G et al., JAMA 2019
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Capillary refill time (CRT) <3 seconds is a valid resuscitation target alongside lactate

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: PCT-guided de-escalation reduces antibiotic exposure without increasing mortality
evidenceBasedPearls.source: PRORATA, de Jong E et al., Lancet Infect Dis 2016
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use PCT trends to guide antibiotic discontinuation - stop if PCT <0.5 or drops >80%
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Antibiotic-free days: 14.3 (PCT-guided) vs 11.6 (standard) with no mortality difference

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Delayed source control (>12h) in septic shock increases mortality
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Azuhata T et al., Crit Care 2014
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Surgical/interventional source control should occur within 6-12 hours when indicated
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Mortality doubled (33% vs 17%) when source control delayed >12 hours

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: qSOFA better predicts mortality than SIRS in non-ICU patients with suspected infection
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use qSOFA (RR ≥22, AMS, SBP ≤100) for bedside screening; SOFA for ICU prognostication
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: qSOFA ≥2: In-hospital mortality 24.4% vs 3.1% if qSOFA 0-1

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: Conservative Fluid Strategy After Initial Resuscitation
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Fluid overload associated with increased mortality in sepsis
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: After initial 30 mL/kg, use dynamic measures (pulse pressure variation, passive leg raise) to guide further fluids
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Conservative strategy improved ventilator-free days (14.6 vs 12.1) without increased renal failure

qsofa.description: Bedside screening tool for sepsis outside the ICU

sofa.description: Assesses organ dysfunction severity in sepsis

patientBackground.recentEvents: Dorothy was her usual self last week - playing cards with friends at the senior center and tending her garden. Three days ago she mentioned to her daughter that it burned when she urinated. Yesterday she became confused and could not remember what day it was. This morning she would not wake up normally.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Had a UTI 2 months ago that was treated with antibiotics. Daughter thinks she might not have finished all the pills. Otherwise has been healthy for her age. Has a history of recurrent UTIs.
patientBackground.fluidIntake: Daughter says she has not been drinking much for the past 2 days. She kept saying she was not thirsty. Maybe a few sips of water and some juice.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Lives in an assisted living facility where she has her own apartment. Staff checks on her twice daily. Daughter visits every weekend. She values her independence highly.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Daughter Beth is healthcare proxy and very involved in her care. Son lives out of state but is being updated. Staff at assisted living know her well and recognized something was wrong.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Dorothy has always trusted doctors and follows medical advice. She is a practical woman who does not complain much. Her main priority is staying in her apartment rather than moving to a nursing home.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Has been to ER a few times in past years - once for a fall, once for pneumonia 18 months ago. This is the sickest her daughter has ever seen her.
patientBackground.occupation: Retired bookkeeper. Worked for a small business for 40 years. Husband passed 10 years ago. Stays busy with activities at the senior center.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if no source was identified?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same septic shock presentation but workup shows no clear source

chiefComplaint.original: She's been confused since yesterday and had a fever last night
chiefComplaint.modified: She's been confused since yesterday and had a fever. No urinary symptoms, no cough, no abdominal pain.

feedback.correct: Good systematic approach! When source unclear, consider: endocarditis (echo), spine infection (if any back pain), C. diff, occult abscess (CT A/P), central line infection, meningitis.
feedback.partial: Good concern for occult source. What imaging/tests help find hidden sources?
feedback.incorrect: Source-negative sepsis requires systematic search: TTE/TEE for endocarditis, CT abdomen/pelvis for abscess, LP if any meningeal signs, spine MRI if back pain, remove/culture all lines.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Clear source (pneumonia, UTI, cellulitis) = targeted workup. Unknown source = broad workup including TTE, CT A/P, consider LP, remove lines. Empiric antibiotics should cover common sources until identified.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if BP didn't respond to fluids?

chiefComplaint.original: She's been confused since yesterday and had a fever last night
chiefComplaint.modified: She's been confused with fever. We gave 2 liters of fluid but BP is still 82/48.

feedback.correct: Fluid-refractory hypotension = start vasopressors. Norepinephrine is first-line. Consider stress-dose steroids if refractory to pressors.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of refractory shock. What's the first-line vasopressor?
feedback.incorrect: After adequate fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg), persistent hypotension = start NOREPINEPHRINE (first-line). If refractory to norepinephrine, consider adding vasopressin and stress-dose hydrocortisone.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Fluid-responsive = give more fluids, may not need pressors. Fluid-refractory = vasopressors needed. Don't delay pressors waiting for more fluid - start norepinephrine and continue resuscitation.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was hypothermic?

chiefComplaint.original: She's been confused since yesterday and had a fever last night
chiefComplaint.modified: 85-year-old nursing home resident, more confused than usual, temp 95.8°F

feedback.correct: Critical teaching point! Elderly often present with HYPOTHERMIA instead of fever in sepsis. This indicates dysregulated thermoregulation and carries WORSE prognosis.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of atypical presentation. What's the prognostic significance?
feedback.incorrect: Hypothermia in suspected sepsis is NOT reassuring - it's WORSE than fever. Elderly lose ability to mount fever response. Hypothermic sepsis has higher mortality. Don't be reassured by 'no fever.'

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Febrile sepsis = expected inflammatory response. Hypothermic sepsis = dysregulated response, often elderly/immunocompromised, HIGHER mortality. Treat just as aggressively - possibly more so.

guidelineReferences.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
keyPoints: Sepsis is medical emergency - immediate treatment (Best Practice)
Antimicrobials within 1 hour for septic shock (Strong)
30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypoperfusion
Target MAP ≥65 mmHg with norepinephrine first-line
VTE prophylaxis with LMWH unless contraindicated (Strong)

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Bacterial Meningitis Guidelines 2004
guidelineReferences.citation: Tunkel AR, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2004;39(9):1267-1284
keyPoints: Empiric: vancomycin + 3rd-gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone/cefotaxime)
Dexamethasone before or with first antibiotic dose for suspected pneumococcal meningitis
LP should not delay antibiotics if bacterial meningitis strongly suspected

female-22-bacterial.name: Karen Lewis
female-22-bacterial.chiefComplaint: I have the worst headache of my life

missedConsequences.Bacterial Meningitis: Without antibiotics, mortality approaches 100%. Even with treatment, 15-25% mortality. Every hour of delay increases mortality.
missedConsequences.Subarachnoid Hemorrhage: 'Worst headache of my life' - ruptured aneurysm is fatal without intervention.
missedConsequences.Encephalitis: Viral brain parenchyma infection - needs antivirals (HSV) or specific treatment.

commonPitfalls: Delaying antibiotics for CT or LP - give antibiotics IMMEDIATELY if bacterial meningitis suspected
Missing subtle meningismus in elderly or immunocompromised
Forgetting dexamethasone before/with first antibiotic dose
Not covering for Listeria in elderly or immunocompromised (add ampicillin)
Waiting for CT before LP when not indicated (most patients don't need CT first)

mcqs.question: A 22-year-old presents with fever, severe headache, and nuchal rigidity. What is the MOST important immediate action?

options.text: CT head, then LP, then antibiotics based on results
options.explanation: NEVER delay antibiotics for diagnostic tests in suspected bacterial meningitis. Delays of even 1-2 hours increase mortality.

options.text: Blood cultures, then empiric antibiotics and dexamethasone immediately
options.explanation: Get cultures if quick (< 2 min), then immediately give dexamethasone + empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin). LP can follow. Don't delay treatment.

options.text: LP first to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
options.explanation: LP should not delay antibiotics. Give antibiotics first if LP will be delayed. Antibiotics don't sterilize CSF for several hours.

options.text: Start acyclovir for presumed viral meningitis
options.explanation: Acyclovir is for HSV encephalitis, not typical viral meningitis. This presentation (high fever, nuchal rigidity) suggests bacterial - needs ceftriaxone + vancomycin.

mcqs.question: CSF shows: WBC 2,200 (90% PMNs), protein 280, glucose 22 (serum 108). Gram stain shows gram-positive diplococci. What organism is MOST likely?

options.explanation: N. meningitidis is gram-NEGATIVE diplococci (kidney bean shaped).

S. pneumoniae is gram-POSITIVE diplococci (lancet-shaped). Most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults. CSF pattern is classic bacterial: high WBC (PMN predominant), high protein, low glucose.
H. flu is gram-negative coccobacilli. Less common since Hib vaccine.
Listeria is gram-positive ROD, not diplococci. Affects elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant.

allDiagnoses.name: Bacterial Meningitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic triad: Fever, headache, nuchal rigidity. High fever, acutely ill, meningeal signs present.

allDiagnoses.name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
allDiagnoses.rationale: 'Worst headache of life' should trigger consideration of SAH. Usually sudden onset, thunderclap character.

allDiagnoses.name: HSV Encephalitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Altered mental status, fever, focal neuro signs. Requires acyclovir. Consider if personality changes or temporal lobe signs.

allDiagnoses.name: Brain Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fever, headache, focal deficits. May need CT before LP.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Meningitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Often milder, but can look similar to early bacterial. CSF helps differentiate.

allDiagnoses.name: Migraine
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause severe headache but typically no fever or meningismus.

allDiagnoses.name: Sinusitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Headache and fever, but no nuchal rigidity unless complicated.

female-22-bacterial.oldcartsNarrative: "This headache started about 18 hours ago and it's the worst headache I've ever had - like a 10 out of 10. It's all over my head, throbbing. The light hurts my eyes and loud sounds bother me. I've been throwing up - probably 4 or 5 times. The headache came on over a few hours, not suddenly. Nothing makes it better - I tried Tylenol and ibuprofen and they didn't help at all. I also feel really sick overall, like I have the flu. I've been kind of out of it - not thinking as clearly as usual."

historyQuestions.text: Describe this headache
historyQuestions.answer: It's the worst headache of my life. It's all over my head, throbbing, and constant. I can't stand the light - it makes the pain worse. Nothing helps it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 'Worst headache of life' is red flag. Need to consider both meningitis and SAH.

historyQuestions.text: How did the headache start?
historyQuestions.answer: It came on over a few hours yesterday. Wasn't sudden like a thunderclap - more gradual. It's been getting worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset over hours more consistent with meningitis than SAH (which is typically thunderclap, maximal immediately)

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your neck
historyQuestions.answer: It's really stiff. I can't bend my head forward at all. It hurts to try.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nuchal rigidity (stiff neck) is hallmark of meningeal irritation

historyQuestions.text: Have you had fevers?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've been burning up. My roommate said I was hot to touch.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High fever with headache and nuchal rigidity = classic meningitis triad

historyQuestions.text: Does light bother you?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, the light is killing me. I've had the shades down all day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Photophobia is common in meningitis (meningeal irritation)

historyQuestions.text: Have you been confused or acting differently?
historyQuestions.answer: My roommate said I've been kind of out of it - not making sense sometimes. I feel foggy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Altered mental status is concerning. Part of classic triad and indicates severity.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your living situation
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a college student, live in a dorm with a roommate. We share a bathroom with other people on the floor.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Close living quarters (dorms, military barracks) are risk factor for N. meningitidis outbreaks

historyQuestions.text: Any rash?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so... maybe some small spots on my legs? I wasn't really paying attention.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Petechial rash = concerning for meningococcemia (N. meningitidis). Look carefully.

historyQuestions.text: Are you up to date on vaccinations?
historyQuestions.answer: I think so? I got shots before starting college but I'm not sure which ones.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Meningococcal vaccine recommended for college students. May affect pathogen likelihood.

historyQuestions.text: Do you take any medications?
historyQuestions.answer: Just birth control pills. And I've been taking Tylenol and ibuprofen for this headache, but they're not helping at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Identifies no immunosuppression; antipyretics may partially mask fever

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No, no allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone, vancomycin)

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've always been healthy. Never been hospitalized. This is the sickest I've ever been.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms immunocompetence; no need for Listeria coverage (ampicillin)

historyQuestions.text: Has anyone around you been sick?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually, a guy on my floor was diagnosed with some kind of infection a few days ago. They sent out an email but I didn't pay attention to it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dorm contact with diagnosed infection concerning for meningococcal outbreak - prophylaxis for contacts needed

historyQuestions.text: Did you have any cold symptoms before this started?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I had a cold earlier this week - runny nose, sore throat. I thought I was getting over it, then this hit me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Preceding viral URI can damage nasopharyngeal mucosa, allowing bacterial invasion (especially pneumococcus)

historyQuestions.text: Have you been vomiting?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, a lot. I've thrown up maybe 4 or 5 times since last night. Nothing stays down.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea/vomiting common with meningitis and increased intracranial pressure

historyQuestions.text: Any serious illnesses in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: Not really. My parents are healthy. No one's had meningitis or anything like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No immunodeficiency syndromes or complement deficiencies suggested

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke, drink, or use drugs?
historyQuestions.answer: I drink at parties sometimes. I've smoked weed a couple times. No cigarettes or other drugs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Social history - no major immunocompromise from substance use

historyQuestions.text: Any recent travel?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've just been at school. Spring break was a couple weeks ago but I just went home to see my parents.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No travel risk factors for unusual pathogens

historyQuestions.text: Any new sexual partners?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been with my boyfriend for a year. We're both tested and exclusive.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out HIV risk and gonococcal meningitis (rare)

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.reasoning: High fever and tachycardia. Not hypotensive (yet) - early meningococcemia can present with shock.

physicalExam.finding: Toxic-appearing young female, lying in bed with eyes closed, avoiding light. Alert but intermittently confused.
physicalExam.reasoning: Toxic appearance with altered mentation = severe illness. Photophobia evident.

physicalExam.finding: No papilledema on fundoscopy. No sinus tenderness. Dry mucous membranes.
physicalExam.reasoning: No papilledema = LP can be done without CT in this patient (see indications). Dehydration noted.

physicalExam.finding: Marked nuchal rigidity - unable to flex neck passively. Pain with neck movement.
physicalExam.reasoning: Nuchal rigidity = meningeal irritation. Classic sign of meningitis.

physicalExam.finding: Positive bilaterally - pain/resistance with knee extension when hip is flexed to 90 degrees
physicalExam.reasoning: Kernig sign = meningeal irritation. Stretches inflamed meninges.

physicalExam.finding: Positive - passive neck flexion causes involuntary hip and knee flexion
physicalExam.reasoning: Brudzinski sign = meningeal irritation. Both Kernig and Brudzinski positive = strong evidence of meningitis.

physicalExam.finding: Alert but intermittently confused, oriented to person only. Pupils equal and reactive. No focal motor deficits. No papilledema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Encephalopathy without focal signs. No papilledema = CT before LP not required. Focal signs would warrant CT first.

physicalExam.finding: Scattered petechiae on lower extremities (approximately 10-15 small non-blanching spots).
physicalExam.reasoning: Petechiae = ALARMING. Strongly suggests meningococcal disease. Can rapidly progress to purpura fulminans.

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular. No murmurs. Capillary refill 2 seconds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia from fever/sepsis. No murmur argues against endocarditis.

labs.name: Blood Cultures (x2 sets)
labs.result: Drawn before antibiotics (took <2 minutes). Results pending.
labs.reasoning: Draw quickly, then give antibiotics. Positive in 50-70% of bacterial meningitis. Do NOT delay antibiotics for cultures.

treatments.name: Dexamethasone

actualDiagnosis.name: Bacterial Meningitis - Streptococcus pneumoniae
keyFindings: Classic triad: Fever (103.4°F), headache ('worst of life'), nuchal rigidity
Meningeal signs: Positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs
Petechial rash (concerning for meningococcemia, but can occur in pneumococcal too)
College student in dormitory (close living quarters)
CSF: High WBC (2,200) with PMN predominance (90%), high protein (280), low glucose (22)
teachingPoints: Classic triad of meningitis: Fever, headache, nuchal rigidity. All three present in only 44% of adults.
Common pathogens by age: Neonates (GBS, E. coli, Listeria), Children (S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis), Adults (S. pneumoniae #1, N. meningitidis), Elderly (S. pneumoniae, Listeria)
DO NOT delay antibiotics for CT or LP. Give antibiotics within 1 hour. Each hour delay increases mortality.
CT before LP only if: Immunocompromised, CNS disease history, new seizure, papilledema, altered consciousness, focal deficit
CSF patterns: Bacterial (high WBC/PMN, low glucose, high protein), Viral (moderate WBC/lymph, normal glucose, mildly elevated protein), TB/fungal (lymphocyte predominant, very low glucose)
Empiric antibiotics: Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin (add ampicillin if >50yo or immunocompromised for Listeria)
Dexamethasone: Give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose. Improves outcomes, especially in pneumococcal meningitis.
Petechiae/purpura with meningitis = N. meningitidis until proven otherwise. Rapidly progressive.
Close contacts of N. meningitidis need prophylaxis: rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone
Kernig sign: Pain with knee extension when hip flexed. Brudzinski sign: Neck flexion causes hip/knee flexion.

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Guidelines for Bacterial Meningitis in Adults 2004/2017 Update
guidelineReferences.citation: Tunkel AR et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2004;39(9):1267-1284
keyPoints: Empiric therapy: Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin (add Ampicillin if >50yo or immunocompromised)
Dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg q6h x 4 days - give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose
CT before LP only if: immunocompromised, history of CNS disease, new seizure, papilledema, altered consciousness, focal deficit
Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT or LP - mortality increases with each hour delay
Duration: S. pneumoniae 10-14 days, N. meningitidis 7 days

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Meningococcal Disease Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
keyPoints: Immediately reportable to public health
Close contact prophylaxis within 24 hours of case identification
Options: Rifampin 600mg BID x 2 days, Ciprofloxacin 500mg x 1, or Ceftriaxone 250mg IM x 1
Close contacts: household members, roommates, anyone with direct oral secretion exposure
Healthcare workers need prophylaxis only if exposed to respiratory secretions without mask

chiefComplaint.original: I have the worst headache of my life with fever and stiff neck
chiefComplaint.modified: Bad headache with fever and stiff neck, started 2 days after a cold

shouldDrop: Bacterial meningitis (less likely but not excluded)

feedback.correct: Lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose suggests viral meningitis - usually self-limited. BUT consider HSV encephalitis (acyclovir), partially-treated bacterial, and TB/fungal.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of viral pattern. What viral cause requires specific treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Viral meningitis pattern: lymphocyte predominant, normal glucose, mildly elevated protein. Usually enterovirus, self-limited. BUT always consider HSV encephalitis (needs acyclovir) - look for temporal lobe involvement on MRI.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Bacterial = PMN predominant, very low glucose, very high protein - treat aggressively. Viral = lymphocyte predominant, normal glucose - supportive care BUT consider acyclovir for HSV until ruled out.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Bacterial meningitis with Listeria coverage needed

feedback.correct: Elderly/immunocompromised = ADD AMPICILLIN to cover Listeria monocytogenes. Empiric: Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin + AMPICILLIN.
feedback.partial: Good recognition that age changes pathogen spectrum. What organism requires additional coverage?
feedback.incorrect: Age >50, immunocompromised, or pregnant = must add AMPICILLIN for Listeria coverage. Standard ceftriaxone + vancomycin doesn't cover Listeria. Elderly may also present atypically - confusion may be only sign.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Young adult = ceftriaxone + vancomycin (S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis). Age >50/immunocompromised = ADD AMPICILLIN for Listeria. Don't forget this - it's a common testing point.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was spreading purpura?

chiefComplaint.original: I have the worst headache of my life with fever and stiff neck
chiefComplaint.modified: I feel terrible with headache - these spots on my skin appeared in the last few hours and are spreading

feedback.correct: Meningococcemia with purpura fulminans = catastrophic emergency. DIC develops rapidly. Aggressive resuscitation, immediate antibiotics, may need FFP/platelets for DIC. Mortality very high.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of meningococcemia. What coagulopathy is developing?
feedback.incorrect: Rapidly spreading purpura + meningitis = N. meningitidis with DIC (purpura fulminans). This progresses to shock and death within hours. Immediate antibiotics, aggressive resuscitation, treat DIC. Contact prophylaxis for close contacts.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Meningitis without rash = concerning but more time. Meningitis with rapidly spreading purpura = meningococcemia with DIC = hours to live without intervention. Maximum urgency.

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA/ESCMID Guidelines: Acute Uncomplicated Pyelonephritis 2010
guidelineReferences.citation: Gupta K, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(5):e103-e120
keyPoints: ALWAYS obtain urine culture and susceptibility for pyelonephritis (A-III)
OUTPATIENT (if FQ resistance <10%): Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID x 7 days OR Cipro XR 1000mg daily x 7 days OR Levofloxacin 750mg daily x 5 days (A-I/B-II)
If FQ resistance >10%: Give initial ceftriaxone 1g IV or aminoglycoside dose before oral therapy (B-III)
TMP-SMX DS BID x 14 days ONLY if organism known susceptible; if unknown susceptibility, give initial parenteral dose (A-I)
Oral beta-lactams are LESS EFFECTIVE for pyelonephritis - if used, give initial ceftriaxone 1g or aminoglycoside (B-III)
HOSPITALIZED patients: IV fluoroquinolone, aminoglycoside ± ampicillin, extended-spectrum cephalosporin/penicillin ± aminoglycoside, or carbapenem (B-III)
Tailor therapy based on culture results; empiric choice based on local resistance patterns
Duration: 7 days for fluoroquinolones, 10-14 days for beta-lactams and TMP-SMX

guidelineReferences.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
keyPoints: For possible sepsis without shock: rapid assessment of infectious vs noninfectious causes
Antimicrobials within 3 hours if concern for sepsis persists (Weak)
Antimicrobials within 1 hour if septic shock develops (Strong)
30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypoperfusion; use balanced crystalloids
Source control - address obstructing stone or abscess promptly (Best Practice)
Measure and trend serum lactate to guide resuscitation

mcqs.question: What distinguishes pyelonephritis from simple cystitis?

options.explanation: Dysuria occurs in both cystitis and pyelonephritis.

options.text: Fever, flank pain, and systemic symptoms
options.explanation: Pyelonephritis = upper tract infection characterized by fever, CVA tenderness, and systemic illness. Cystitis is lower tract only.
Both conditions have positive urine cultures.
Both conditions are more common in females but gender doesn't distinguish them.

mcqs.question: Which finding on CT would require urologic consultation?

options.explanation: Mild renal enlargement is expected with acute pyelonephritis.
Perinephric fat stranding is common in pyelonephritis and doesn't require intervention.
Abscess may need drainage; obstructing stone with infection is a urologic emergency requiring urgent decompression.
Urothelial thickening is expected with infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Pyelonephritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, dysuria, and pyuria = classic pyelonephritis presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Simple Cystitis (UTI)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Dysuria present but fever and flank pain indicate upper tract involvement beyond simple cystitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Nephrolithiasis with Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Infected stone is urologic emergency. Must rule out with imaging if not improving.

allDiagnoses.name: Perinephric Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Complication of pyelonephritis requiring drainage. Consider if not improving on antibiotics.

allDiagnoses.name: Appendicitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause fever and abdominal pain but location and urinary symptoms point to kidney.

allDiagnoses.name: Ovarian Pathology
allDiagnoses.rationale: PID or tubo-ovarian abscess can cause fever and pelvic/flank pain. Consider in differential.

allDiagnoses.name: Musculoskeletal Back Pain
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not cause fever, dysuria, or pyuria.

female-28-pyelo.oldcartsNarrative: "This started about 2 days ago. I thought I was getting a UTI - burning when I pee, going frequently. I tried drinking cranberry juice and water but it just got worse. Yesterday I started feeling really sick - feverish, body aches, like I have the flu. Then this morning I woke up with this pain in my left side, toward my back. It's a constant, dull aching pain, maybe 7 out of 10. I feel really sick and weak. I've had a few UTIs before but never felt this bad."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your urinary symptoms.
historyQuestions.answer: Burning when I pee, feels like I have to go all the time but only a little comes out. The urine looks cloudy and smells bad.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic lower urinary tract symptoms that preceded upper tract infection.

historyQuestions.text: When did the fever start?
historyQuestions.answer: Yesterday. Started with chills, then felt really hot. I took my temperature and it was 102.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever indicates systemic infection - distinguishes pyelonephritis from simple cystitis.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the back pain.
historyQuestions.answer: It's on my left side, kind of in the back toward my side. Constant aching. Worse when I move around.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Flank pain indicates renal involvement - classic for pyelonephritis.

historyQuestions.text: How are you feeling overall?
historyQuestions.answer: Terrible. Weak, tired, body aches everywhere. No appetite. I feel like I have a bad flu.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Systemic symptoms indicate significant infection beyond localized bladder infection.

historyQuestions.text: Any nausea or vomiting?
historyQuestions.answer: Queasy since yesterday. I threw up once this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea/vomiting common with pyelonephritis. Important for determining oral vs IV antibiotics.

historyQuestions.text: How often do you get UTIs?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe 2-3 times a year. Usually clears up with antibiotics. Never felt this sick before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recurrent UTIs are risk factor. Ascending infection from repeated lower tract infections.

historyQuestions.text: Are you sexually active?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, with my boyfriend. We don't use condoms since I'm on the pill.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sexual activity is risk factor for UTI in women ('honeymoon cystitis').

historyQuestions.text: Is there any chance you could be pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so. I'm on the pill and had my period two weeks ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must rule out pregnancy - pyelonephritis in pregnancy requires inpatient management.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 2 weeks ago. It was normal - 5 days, regular flow. I'm pretty regular on the pill.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent normal period and OCP use make pregnancy unlikely but must test

historyQuestions.text: What kind of birth control do you use?
historyQuestions.answer: Oral contraceptive pills. I've been on them for a few years. I'm good about taking them every day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Contraceptive method for pregnancy risk assessment

historyQuestions.text: Any unusual vaginal discharge?
historyQuestions.answer: No discharge. That all seems normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Helps differentiate from PID or tubo-ovarian abscess

historyQuestions.text: Do your UTIs seem related to sexual activity?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, actually they often happen a day or two after we're together. I've noticed that pattern.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Post-coital UTIs are common - opportunity for prevention counseling

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before prescribing antibiotics

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just my birth control pill. I tried taking some leftover antibiotics when this started but I only had a couple pills.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Incomplete antibiotic course may have selected for resistant organisms

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. Just these recurrent UTIs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms uncomplicated host - no diabetes, immunocompromise, or structural abnormalities

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_sexual_activity.answer: Sexually active with one partner. We're pretty active - maybe 3-4 times a week.
social_sexual_activity.relevance: Sexual activity is major risk factor for UTI in women - mechanical introduction of bacteria.

social_voiding_habits.answer: I don't always pee after sex. I know I'm supposed to but sometimes I'm too tired.
social_voiding_habits.relevance: Post-coital voiding helps flush bacteria from urethra - key prevention strategy.

diet_hydration.answer: I probably don't drink enough water. Mostly coffee and diet soda.
diet_hydration.relevance: Inadequate hydration means less frequent voiding and bacterial flushing.

occupation_bathroom_access.answer: I'm a teacher. Hard to leave the classroom to use the bathroom. I hold it a lot.
occupation_bathroom_access.relevance: Holding urine allows bacterial multiplication. Important modifiable risk factor.

social_contraception.answer: Just birth control pills. We stopped using condoms a few months ago.
social_contraception.relevance: Spermicides increase UTI risk; oral contraceptives alone don't but pregnancy must be ruled out.

hygiene_wiping_habits.answer: I wipe front to back like you're supposed to.
hygiene_wiping_habits.relevance: Proper wiping technique reduces bacterial introduction - good practice maintained.

social_clothing.answer: I wear thongs mostly. Tight leggings a lot for yoga.
social_clothing.relevance: Tight clothing and thong underwear may contribute to bacterial transfer though evidence is limited.

diet_cranberry_use.answer: I drink cranberry juice when I feel a UTI coming on.
diet_cranberry_use.relevance: Cranberry may have modest preventive effect but doesn't treat established infection.

physicalExam.finding: BP 108/68, HR 108 (tachycardic), RR 18, Temp 102.4°F (febrile), SpO2 98%.
physicalExam.reasoning: Fever and tachycardia indicate systemic infection. Monitor for sepsis criteria.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing young woman, flushed, mildly diaphoretic. Appears uncomfortable but not toxic.
physicalExam.reasoning: Systemically ill but not in septic shock. Can likely tolerate outpatient treatment if improving.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness. No rebound or guarding. No masses.
physicalExam.reasoning: Suprapubic tenderness consistent with bladder inflammation. No peritonitis.

physicalExam.finding: Marked LEFT CVA tenderness. Patient winces and pulls away. Right side non-tender.
physicalExam.reasoning: CVA tenderness = HALLMARK of pyelonephritis. Indicates renal involvement.
