labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Cloudy, yellow. WBC: >100/hpf (MANY). RBC: 10-20/hpf. Nitrite: positive. Leukocyte esterase: positive. Bacteria: many.
labs.reasoning: Classic pyuria with positive nitrite and LE. Confirms bacterial infection. WBC casts would confirm pyelonephritis but not always seen.

labs.name: Urine Culture with Sensitivities
labs.result: Pending - preliminary: gram-negative rods >100,000 CFU/mL. Final: E. coli, susceptible to ciprofloxacin, TMP-SMX, ceftriaxone.
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL - identifies organism and sensitivities. E. coli causes 80% of pyelonephritis. MUST follow up culture to ensure appropriate coverage.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 15.2 (elevated) with left shift (85% neutrophils, 10% bands). Hgb 13.2, Plt 285.
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with left shift (bandemia) indicates bacterial infection. Helps assess severity.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 138, K 3.8, Cl 102, CO2 22, BUN 20, Cr 0.9, Glucose 108. All normal.
labs.reasoning: Normal renal function - no AKI from infection or obstruction. Baseline for antibiotic dosing.

labs.name: Urine Pregnancy Test
labs.reasoning: MANDATORY in reproductive-age women. Pyelonephritis in pregnancy requires admission - high risk for preterm labor and sepsis.

labs.name: Blood Cultures x 2 (if septic or admission)
labs.result: Obtained. No growth at 48 hours. Final negative.
labs.reasoning: Draw if patient appears septic or requires admission. Positive in 20-30% of pyelonephritis cases. Guides therapy if organism differs from urine.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Normal lactate argues against sepsis with organ dysfunction. Elevated lactate would indicate need for aggressive resuscitation.

labs.name: Procalcitonin (if diagnostic uncertainty)
labs.reasoning: Can help differentiate bacterial vs viral infection. Not needed when diagnosis is clinically apparent.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast
imaging.result: Left kidney mildly enlarged with perinephric fat stranding. No abscess or fluid collection. No stones. No hydronephrosis. Right kidney normal.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms pyelonephritis (stranding), rules out abscess and obstructing stone. NOT always needed - reserve for: (1) not improving on antibiotics at 48-72 hours, (2) concern for abscess/stone, (3) atypical presentation.

imaging.name: Renal Ultrasound
imaging.result: Left kidney slightly enlarged. No hydronephrosis. No stones. No abscess visualized. Right kidney normal.
imaging.reasoning: Can rule out obstruction (hydronephrosis) and stones. Less sensitive for abscess than CT. First-line if pregnant or avoiding contrast.

imaging.name: No Imaging (for uncomplicated cases)
imaging.result: Imaging not routinely required for uncomplicated pyelonephritis in healthy young women responding to treatment.
imaging.reasoning: ACOG and IDSA guidelines do not require imaging for uncomplicated pyelonephritis in young, healthy, non-pregnant women who respond to antibiotics within 48-72 hours.

procedures.name: IV Access
procedures.result: 20-gauge IV placed for fluids and IV antibiotic.
procedures.reasoning: IV access for hydration and initial IV antibiotic dose, even if planning outpatient management.

procedures.name: Urology Drainage Procedure (if obstructing stone)
procedures.result: Not indicated - no obstruction on imaging.
procedures.reasoning: EMERGENT urology if obstructing stone with infection - requires urgent decompression with ureteral stent or nephrostomy tube. Antibiotics alone cannot clear infected obstructed system.

treatments.name: IV Fluid Resuscitation
treatments.result: NS 1L bolus given. Maintenance fluids for hydration.
treatments.reasoning: Patients are often dehydrated from fever, poor intake, and vomiting. Fluid resuscitation improves renal perfusion and antibiotic delivery.

treatments.name: Ceftriaxone 1g IV x 1 dose (initial)
treatments.result: Ceftriaxone 1g IV given in ED. Single dose for transition to oral therapy.
treatments.reasoning: IV cephalosporin provides reliable initial coverage. Even outpatients often receive one IV dose before transitioning to oral. Covers most uropathogens.

treatments.name: Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID x 7 days (OR Levofloxacin 750mg daily x 5 days)
treatments.result: Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID prescribed for 7-day course. Start after IV dose.
treatments.reasoning: Fluoroquinolones are FIRST-LINE oral therapy for outpatient pyelonephritis. Excellent kidney penetration. 7-day course for uncomplicated pyelo. Check local resistance patterns - if FQ resistance >10%, use alternative.

treatments.name: TMP-SMX DS BID x 14 days (alternative)
treatments.result: Alternative option if culture shows susceptibility and FQ contraindicated.
treatments.reasoning: Second-line option. Requires 14 days (longer than FQ). ONLY use if culture confirms susceptibility - resistance is common.

treatments.name: Ondansetron 4mg IV/PO
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV given for nausea. Prescription for home use.
treatments.reasoning: Control nausea to ensure patient can tolerate oral antibiotics and fluids. Key for successful outpatient management.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen/Ibuprofen
treatments.result: Acetaminophen 1g PO q6h PRN fever/pain. Ibuprofen 600mg q8h PRN as alternative.
treatments.reasoning: Fever control improves comfort. NSAIDs also provide analgesia. Avoid NSAIDs if any concern for AKI.

treatments.name: UTI Prevention Counseling
treatments.result: Counseled on prevention: post-coital voiding, adequate hydration (2L/day), avoid holding urine, wiping front-to-back.
treatments.reasoning: Recurrent UTI prevention is important. Post-coital voiding has modest evidence. Hydration helps dilute bacteria. Consider prophylaxis if ≥3 UTIs/year.

consults.name: Urology (if complicated)
consults.result: Not indicated for uncomplicated pyelonephritis.
consults.reasoning: Urology consult for: obstructing stone with infection (EMERGENCY), recurrent pyelonephritis, anatomic abnormalities, perinephric abscess requiring drainage.

consults.name: Infectious Disease (if MDR organism or failure)
consults.result: Not indicated for uncomplicated case with susceptible organism.
consults.reasoning: ID consult for: MDR organism (ESBL), treatment failure, recurrent complicated infections, or immunocompromised host.

consults.name: OB/GYN (if pregnant)
consults.result: Not applicable - patient not pregnant.
consults.reasoning: Pregnant patients with pyelonephritis require OB involvement - high risk for preterm labor. Always requires admission in pregnancy.

disposition.name: Outpatient Management (if criteria met)
disposition.result: Discharged home after IV fluid, one IV antibiotic dose, and demonstrated ability to tolerate oral intake. 7-day oral FQ course.
disposition.reasoning: Outpatient criteria: (1) Not pregnant, (2) No vomiting/able to tolerate PO, (3) Not septic, (4) No obstruction/abscess, (5) Able to follow up. This patient meets all criteria.

disposition.name: Admission Criteria (when needed)
disposition.result: Would admit if: sepsis/hemodynamic instability, persistent vomiting, pregnant, obstructing stone, abscess, immunocompromised, failed outpatient therapy, unreliable follow-up.
disposition.reasoning: Know admission indications. Pregnant patients ALWAYS admitted. Infected obstructing stone is urologic EMERGENCY requiring drainage.

disposition.name: Follow-up Plan
disposition.result: Follow up in 2-3 days if not improving. Phone call with culture results in 48-72 hours. Return precautions given.
disposition.reasoning: Must follow up culture results - if organism resistant to empiric therapy, need to change antibiotics. Not improving in 48-72 hours warrants imaging for abscess/obstruction.

disposition.name: Return Precautions
disposition.result: Return if: fever >103°F, worsening pain, unable to keep down fluids/meds, new confusion, not improving in 2-3 days.
disposition.reasoning: Patient education critical for safe outpatient management. Worsening could indicate abscess, resistant organism, or developing sepsis.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Pyelonephritis
teachingPoints: CVA tenderness + fever + pyuria = pyelonephritis until proven otherwise
Fever distinguishes pyelonephritis from simple cystitis - key clinical decision point
Fluoroquinolones or TMP-SMX are first-line for uncomplicated pyelo; 7-14 days of treatment
Admit if: sepsis, unable to tolerate PO, pregnant, obstructing stone, immunocompromised
Always follow urine culture - resistance to empiric antibiotics is increasingly common
If not improving in 48-72 hours, image to look for abscess or obstruction

closureScript.diagnosis: You have pyelonephritis - a kidney infection. What started as a bladder infection has spread up to your left kidney. That's why you have the high fever, flank pain, and feel so sick.
closureScript.plan: I'm going to start you on a strong antibiotic that will treat this infection. We'll give you IV fluids and medication for nausea. If you're able to keep fluids down and your fever comes down, you can go home with oral antibiotics. The full course is 7-10 days. We'll call you with your urine culture results to make sure the antibiotic is the right one.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back or call if your fever goes over 103, you can't keep fluids or medications down, the pain gets worse, you feel confused, or you're not improving in 2-3 days.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You should start feeling better within 24-48 hours of starting antibiotics. We'll also talk about ways to prevent future UTIs - things like staying hydrated, urinating after sex, and not holding your urine too long.

allDiagnoses.name: Enteric Fever (paratyphoid)

male-28-vfr.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this fever for about 2 weeks now. It started low and has gotten higher every day - like climbing stairs. Now it's constant around 103. I have a terrible headache, my stomach hurts, and I'm exhausted. At first I was constipated for days, but now I have diarrhea. I noticed some small pink spots on my chest that come and go. I just got back from visiting family overseas about 2 weeks ago."

historyQuestions.text: Describe your fever pattern
historyQuestions.answer: It started low and got higher every day, like climbing stairs. Now it's constant around 103.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Stepladder fever pattern is classic for typhoid

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your stomach symptoms
historyQuestions.answer: First I was constipated for days. Now I have watery diarrhea and belly pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Constipation → diarrhea progression is classic for typhoid ('pea soup stools')

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the spots you noticed
historyQuestions.answer: Small pink spots on my chest and stomach. They appear and fade. Not itchy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rose spots are pathognomonic for typhoid - appear in 2nd week, transient

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your recent travel
historyQuestions.answer: I visited family in Pakistan for 3 weeks. Got back about 12 days ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pakistan is endemic for typhoid. VFR travel is highest risk.

historyQuestions.text: Did you get any travel vaccines?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I didn't get any vaccines. I've been going to Pakistan my whole life without problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No typhoid vaccine + endemic area = high risk. VFR travelers often skip vaccines.

historyQuestions.text: What did you eat and drink there?
historyQuestions.answer: We ate home-cooked food at my grandmother's. Drank tap water there.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Contaminated water/food is transmission route for Salmonella typhi

historyQuestions.text: Was anyone else sick?
historyQuestions.answer: My cousin who I stayed with had similar fever last month.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Typhoid spreads in households. Cousin may have been source.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing. I'm usually healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review for treatment planning

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before starting antibiotics - fluoroquinolones or azithromycin

historyQuestions.text: Any medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been healthy. Never been hospitalized.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for severity assessment and treatment decisions

historyQuestions.text: Did you take any malaria prevention medication?
historyQuestions.answer: No, no one mentioned that to me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No malaria prophylaxis increases risk - must check smear

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

geographic_international_travel.answer: Pakistan for 3 weeks. Stayed with family in a small city.
geographic_international_travel.relevance: Pakistan is endemic for typhoid. VFR (visiting friends and relatives) travelers are at highest risk because they stay longer, eat local food, and often skip pre-travel vaccines.

geographic_vfr_travel.answer: Yes, stayed with grandparents and aunts/uncles. Felt just like home.
geographic_vfr_travel.relevance: VFR travelers have 8x higher risk of travel-related illness than tourists because they eat home-cooked food, drink local water, and have prolonged close contact.

water_food_water_source.answer: Drank tap water at grandmother's house. She boils it sometimes.
water_food_water_source.relevance: Contaminated water is the primary transmission route for Salmonella typhi. 'Sometimes' boiling is not protective.

water_food_raw_foods.answer: Lots of fresh fruits and salads. My aunt made fresh juices every day.
water_food_raw_foods.relevance: Raw produce washed in contaminated water transmits typhoid. Fresh juices may be diluted with contaminated water.

social_household_others_sick.answer: My cousin had similar fever last month.
social_household_others_sick.relevance: Typhoid spreads in households. Cousin may have been the source or another victim of common exposure.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing, toxic, lying still in bed
physicalExam.reasoning: Toxic appearance indicates serious systemic illness

physicalExam.finding: BP 108/68, HR 72, Temp 103.2°F - NOTE: RELATIVE BRADYCARDIA
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation) is classic for typhoid. HR should be ~110 at this temp.

physicalExam.finding: Rose spots: 2-4mm salmon-colored blanching macules on upper abdomen and lower chest
physicalExam.reasoning: Rose spots are PATHOGNOMONIC for typhoid fever

physicalExam.finding: Distended, diffusely tender. Hepatosplenomegaly present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hepatosplenomegaly is common in typhoid

physicalExam.finding: Alert but slow to respond, appears slightly confused - 'typhoid state'
physicalExam.reasoning: Encephalopathy ('typhoid state') occurs in severe cases

labs.name: Blood cultures x2
labs.result: Pending - Salmonella typhi isolated on day 3
labs.reasoning: Blood culture is diagnostic gold standard - 60-80% sensitive in week 1. Sensitivity decreases in later weeks.

labs.name: Stool culture
labs.reasoning: Stool culture becomes positive later in illness (week 2-3). Also important for detecting carrier state.

labs.name: Bone marrow culture (if blood negative)
labs.reasoning: Bone marrow culture is most sensitive (90%) but invasive. Reserve for culture-negative cases with high clinical suspicion.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Leukopenia (not leukocytosis!) is characteristic of typhoid. Anemia and thrombocytopenia common.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.reasoning: Mild hepatitis common in typhoid. Marked elevation suggests complications.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Electrolyte abnormalities common. Monitor renal function.

labs.name: Malaria thick and thin smear
labs.reasoning: MUST rule out malaria in any febrile traveler from endemic area. Can co-exist with typhoid.

labs.name: Widal test (serologic)
labs.reasoning: Widal test has poor sensitivity and specificity. Not recommended by current guidelines. Cultures are preferred.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Normal
imaging.reasoning: Rule out other causes of fever. Pneumonia can occur as complication.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray (upright/supine)
imaging.result: No free air. Nonspecific bowel gas pattern.
imaging.reasoning: Baseline imaging. Watch for free air indicating intestinal perforation - occurs in 2nd-3rd week.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen (if perforation suspected)
imaging.result: Not indicated currently.
imaging.reasoning: Reserve for suspected complications: perforation, abscess, cholecystitis.

treatments.name: Azithromycin 1g PO daily x 5-7 days (FIRST-LINE if resistant)
treatments.result: Azithromycin 1g PO daily started empirically pending culture sensitivities.
treatments.reasoning: Azithromycin is first-line for MDR typhoid (common from South Asia). Fluoroquinolone resistance >80% in Pakistan/India.

treatments.name: Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily x 10-14 days (alternative)
treatments.result: Alternative for severe disease or if unable to tolerate oral.
treatments.reasoning: IV ceftriaxone for severe typhoid or patients unable to take oral medications. Good for MDR strains.

treatments.name: Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID (if susceptible)
treatments.result: Avoid empirically - high resistance rates from South Asia (>80% in Pakistan/India).
treatments.reasoning: Fluoroquinolones were first-line but resistance now widespread. Only use if susceptibility confirmed on culture.

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: NS maintenance fluids. Bolus for hypotension.
treatments.reasoning: Fluid resuscitation for dehydration from fever and diarrhea. Monitor for fluid overload.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen for fever
treatments.result: Acetaminophen 650mg q6h PRN for fever and comfort.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic fever control. Avoid NSAIDs (bleeding risk with thrombocytopenia).

treatments.name: Dexamethasone (for severe typhoid with shock/encephalopathy)
treatments.result: Not indicated - patient not in shock or obtunded.
treatments.reasoning: High-dose dexamethasone reduces mortality in severe typhoid with shock or altered mental status (classic study by Hoffman). Not for routine cases.

consults.name: Infectious Disease consult
consults.result: Recommends empiric azithromycin pending cultures given high FQ resistance from Pakistan. Will assist with antibiotic de-escalation.
consults.reasoning: ID expertise critical for antibiotic selection given regional resistance patterns. Also for public health reporting.

consults.name: Surgery consult (if perforation suspected)
consults.result: Not indicated currently. Would consult urgently for signs of perforation.
consults.reasoning: Surgery needed for intestinal perforation - a feared complication in weeks 2-3. Watch for sudden worsening abdominal pain, rigidity, free air.

disposition.name: Admit to medicine
disposition.result: Admitted to medicine floor for IV fluids, antibiotics, and monitoring for complications.
disposition.reasoning: Requires admission for treatment and to watch for complications (perforation, hemorrhage, encephalopathy).

disposition.name: Contact precautions
disposition.result: Contact precautions for duration of illness. Hand hygiene critical.
disposition.reasoning: Typhoid is transmitted fecal-oral. Contact precautions prevent nosocomial spread.

disposition.name: Complication monitoring
disposition.result: Daily abdominal exams. Watch for: sudden abdominal pain (perforation), GI bleeding, altered mental status, worsening fever.
disposition.reasoning: Intestinal perforation occurs in 1-3% of cases, usually in week 2-3. GI hemorrhage in 10%. Both are surgical emergencies.

disposition.name: Public health reporting
disposition.result: Typhoid is reportable. Notified local health department.
disposition.reasoning: Typhoid fever is a reportable disease. Public health will investigate source and contacts.

disposition.name: Post-treatment stool cultures (carrier screening)
disposition.result: Will need stool cultures after treatment to confirm clearance. Some patients become chronic carriers.
disposition.reasoning: 1-4% become chronic carriers (>1 year stool shedding). Important for food handlers. May need prolonged antibiotics or cholecystectomy for carriers.

disposition.name: Household contact screening
disposition.result: Household contacts should be monitored for symptoms. Consider stool cultures.
disposition.reasoning: Household transmission common. Contacts may be infected or carriers.

disposition.name: Return precautions (if treated outpatient)
disposition.result: This patient admitted. Outpatients would return for: worsening abdominal pain, bloody stool, persistent high fever after 5 days of antibiotics, confusion.
disposition.reasoning: Complications typically occur in weeks 2-3. Must educate about perforation signs.

actualDiagnosis.name: Typhoid Fever (Salmonella typhi)
keyFindings: Ate local food and drank tap water
teachingPoints: VFR travelers are highest risk - they skip vaccines and eat local food
Stepladder fever + relative bradycardia + rose spots = typhoid until proven otherwise
Rose spots appear in second week, last only hours, easy to miss
Constipation early, diarrhea late ('pea soup stools')
Blood culture positive week 1; stool culture positive weeks 2-3
WATCH FOR INTESTINAL PERFORATION - surgical emergency (usually week 3)
Fluoroquinolone resistance is increasing - check local patterns
Screen for chronic carrier state after treatment

closureScript.diagnosis: You have typhoid fever - a bacterial infection you almost certainly picked up from contaminated food or water in Pakistan. This is a serious but treatable infection.
closureScript.plan: We're admitting you to the hospital for IV antibiotics. You should start feeling better within a few days, but you'll need to complete a full course of antibiotics. We need to watch you closely for complications.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: The main complication we watch for is intestinal perforation, which usually happens in the third week. If you develop severe abdominal pain, we need to know immediately. After you recover, we'll test to make sure you're not carrying the bacteria - some people become chronic carriers.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: For future trips to Pakistan, please get the typhoid vaccine beforehand. Also, even at your grandmother's house, it's best to drink only bottled or boiled water and be careful with raw foods. Your family members who visited should watch for symptoms too.

allDiagnoses.name: Lyme Disease

allDiagnoses.name: Cellulitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cellulitis doesn't have central clearing or multiple identical lesions.

allDiagnoses.name: Reactive Arthritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not explain the rash.

allDiagnoses.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis

female-34-lyme.oldcartsNarrative: "About two weeks ago I noticed a red ring on my thigh that has been slowly expanding. It started small and now it's huge - maybe 6 inches across. The center is lighter colored. A few days ago I noticed two more similar rings on my back. I've also been feeling tired, achy, and my right knee got swollen and painful. I spent a weekend hiking in Connecticut about three weeks ago. I don't remember getting bitten by a tick but I wasn't really checking."

historyQuestions.text: Describe the rash in detail
historyQuestions.answer: It's a ring - red on the outside, clearer in the middle. Started as a small red spot and expanded outward. Now about 6 inches across.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CLASSIC erythema migrans (EM) - 'bull's eye' or target lesion is pathognomonic

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned more lesions on your back?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, two more rings appeared on my back a few days ago. Smaller but same pattern.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multiple EM lesions indicate DISSEMINATED disease - spirochetes have spread

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your knee
historyQuestions.answer: Right knee got swollen and painful about a week ago. Hard to bend it all the way.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Lyme arthritis - indicates early disseminated disease

historyQuestions.text: Any other symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: Tired, achy all over, some headaches. Feels like mild flu that won't go away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Constitutional symptoms common in early disseminated Lyme

historyQuestions.text: Did you notice a tick bite?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I never saw a tick. But I was hiking through tall grass and didn't check myself well.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Most patients don't recall tick bite - nymph ticks are tiny (poppy seed size)

historyQuestions.text: Any palpitations, chest pain, or facial weakness?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screening for cardiac and neurologic Lyme - not yet present

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Birth control pills. That's it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Doxycycline (Lyme treatment) can reduce OCP efficacy - need to counsel

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important - doxycycline is first-line treatment

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Healthy. No medical problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Baseline health status

historyQuestions.text: Any chance you could be pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm on the pill and had my period last week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical - doxycycline contraindicated in pregnancy; would need amoxicillin

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

geographic_recent_travel.answer: Went hiking in Connecticut for a long weekend, about 3 weeks ago.
geographic_recent_travel.relevance: Connecticut is in the heart of Lyme-endemic area (named for Lyme, CT). Peak transmission May-August.

hobbies_hiking_camping.answer: I love hiking. We went off-trail through grassy meadows and wooded areas.
hobbies_hiking_camping.relevance: Off-trail hiking through grass and brush = high tick exposure. Ixodes ticks live in leaf litter and tall grass.

hobbies_gardening.answer: Yes, I garden at home in my backyard which has woods behind it.
hobbies_gardening.relevance: Suburban-woodland interface is prime deer tick habitat. Could have local exposure too.

social_household_pets_home.answer: We have a dog who goes on hikes with us.
social_household_pets_home.relevance: Dogs can carry ticks into home. Dog should also be checked and on tick prevention.

geographic_places_lived.answer: I live in suburban New Jersey, near woods.
geographic_places_lived.relevance: New Jersey is also highly endemic for Lyme disease.

physicalExam.finding: 15cm erythematous annular plaque with central clearing on left lateral thigh. Classic 'bull's eye' appearance.
physicalExam.reasoning: ERYTHEMA MIGRANS - this finding alone is diagnostic of Lyme. No testing needed.

physicalExam.finding: Two additional EM lesions on back, 5-6cm each, similar target appearance
physicalExam.reasoning: Multiple EM = DISSEMINATED Lyme. Requires longer antibiotic course.

physicalExam.finding: Right knee warm, swollen, moderate effusion. Painful range of motion.
physicalExam.reasoning: Lyme arthritis - monoarticular large joint involvement is classic

physicalExam.finding: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Normal heart sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: No evidence of Lyme carditis (would hear irregularity with heart block)

physicalExam.finding: Cranial nerves intact. No facial weakness. No meningismus.
physicalExam.reasoning: No evidence of neurologic Lyme (Bell's palsy, meningitis)

labs.name: Lyme serology (ELISA/Western blot)
labs.result: May be negative in early disease. DO NOT wait for results to treat classic EM.
labs.reasoning: Two-tier testing: ELISA first, if positive/equivocal → Western blot. Serology often negative in first 2-4 weeks. EM rash alone is diagnostic - treat empirically without waiting for serology.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Usually normal in Lyme. Mild lymphocytosis may occur.

labs.name: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel
labs.reasoning: Baseline before antibiotic therapy. Elevated LFTs occasionally seen in disseminated Lyme.

labs.name: ECG
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Screen for Lyme carditis. Heart block (prolonged PR, AV block) occurs in 1-5% of untreated cases. If PR >300ms or high-grade block → admission for IV antibiotics and monitoring.

labs.name: Lumbar puncture (if neuro symptoms)
labs.reasoning: LP indicated if: facial palsy, meningitis symptoms, radiculopathy. Would show lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, positive Lyme antibody index.

imaging.name: Knee X-ray
imaging.result: Effusion present. No bony abnormalities, no erosions.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out other joint pathology. Lyme arthritis doesn't cause erosions (unlike RA).

imaging.name: Knee ultrasound (if arthrocentesis planned)
imaging.result: Moderate effusion confirmed. Can guide aspiration.
imaging.reasoning: Only if planning arthrocentesis or diagnosis unclear.

imaging.name: MRI Knee
imaging.result: Not indicated for typical Lyme arthritis.
imaging.reasoning: Reserve for atypical presentations or treatment failure.

procedures.name: Knee arthrocentesis
procedures.result: Inflammatory fluid - WBC 25,000 with lymphocyte predominance. Gram stain negative. Culture negative.
procedures.reasoning: Can confirm inflammatory arthritis and rule out septic joint. Not strictly necessary with classic EM rash. Lyme arthritis fluid: WBC 10,000-25,000 (lymphocyte predominant).

treatments.name: Doxycycline 100mg PO BID x 21 days (FIRST-LINE)
treatments.result: Doxycycline 100mg BID for 21 days prescribed. Counsel to take with food, avoid dairy, use sun protection.
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE for early disseminated Lyme. 21 days for disseminated (vs 10-14 for localized EM only). Covers co-infections (Anaplasma). Avoid in pregnancy, children <8.

treatments.name: Amoxicillin 500mg PO TID x 21 days (alternative)
treatments.result: Alternative if doxycycline contraindicated (pregnancy, age <8, allergy).
treatments.reasoning: Use in pregnancy, children <8, or doxycycline allergy. Same duration as doxycycline. Does NOT cover Anaplasma co-infection.

treatments.name: Cefuroxime 500mg PO BID x 21 days (alternative)
treatments.result: Alternative second-line oral option.
treatments.reasoning: Second-line alternative. Use if both doxycycline and amoxicillin contraindicated.

treatments.name: NSAIDs for arthritis symptoms
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 600mg TID PRN for knee pain and swelling.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic relief for Lyme arthritis. Anti-inflammatory effect helps with joint swelling.

treatments.name: IV Ceftriaxone (if cardiac/neuro involvement)
treatments.result: Not indicated - no cardiac or neurologic Lyme.
treatments.reasoning: IV antibiotics reserved for: Lyme carditis with AV block, Lyme meningitis, or severe neurologic Lyme. This patient has only arthritis + skin - oral is adequate.

treatments.name: OCP + Doxycycline counseling
treatments.result: Counseled that doxycycline may reduce oral contraceptive efficacy. Use backup contraception during treatment and 7 days after.
treatments.reasoning: Drug interaction - antibiotics (especially doxycycline) may reduce OCP efficacy. Critical counseling point for women of reproductive age.

consults.name: Infectious Disease (if complicated)
consults.result: Not needed for straightforward early disseminated Lyme.
consults.reasoning: ID consult for: treatment failure, atypical presentation, Lyme carditis/meningitis, or post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome.

consults.name: Cardiology (if heart block)
consults.result: Not indicated - normal ECG.
consults.reasoning: Cardiology for Lyme carditis with significant conduction abnormalities. May need temporary pacemaker for high-grade AV block.

disposition.name: Outpatient treatment with oral antibiotics
disposition.result: Discharged with 21-day doxycycline course. Follow-up in 2-3 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Early disseminated Lyme WITHOUT cardiac or neurologic involvement can be treated outpatient with oral antibiotics. This patient qualifies.

disposition.name: Admission criteria
disposition.result: Would admit for: symptomatic heart block, severe neurologic involvement, unable to tolerate oral medications.
disposition.reasoning: Know when to admit: PR >300ms, second/third-degree AV block, meningitis, severe radiculopathy. This patient doesn't meet criteria.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: Return in 2-3 weeks to assess response. Rash and constitutional symptoms should improve within days. Arthritis may take weeks to fully resolve.
disposition.reasoning: Most patients improve significantly within 1-2 weeks. Arthritis may take 4-6 weeks. If no improvement, consider treatment failure or PTLDS.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return if: palpitations, chest pain, dizziness/syncope, facial weakness, severe headache with stiff neck, worsening symptoms.
disposition.reasoning: Cardiac and neurologic Lyme can develop during treatment. Patient must know warning signs.

disposition.name: Tick prevention counseling
disposition.result: Counseled on: tick checks after outdoor activities, DEET repellent, permethrin-treated clothing, prompt tick removal, protecting the dog.
disposition.reasoning: Prevention education reduces future risk. Prior Lyme does not provide immunity - can be reinfected.

disposition.name: Post-treatment expectations
disposition.result: Counseled that some fatigue/achiness may persist weeks after treatment. This is NOT indication for prolonged antibiotics.
disposition.reasoning: Post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS) occurs in 10-20%. Prolonged antibiotics not beneficial and potentially harmful.

actualDiagnosis.name: Early Disseminated Lyme Disease
keyFindings: Hiking in Connecticut (endemic area) 3 weeks ago
Classic erythema migrans rash with central clearing
teachingPoints: Erythema migrans is DIAGNOSTIC - treat without waiting for serology
Serology is often NEGATIVE in first 2 weeks
Multiple EM lesions = disseminated disease, needs 21-day treatment
Endemic areas: Northeast, upper Midwest, Pacific coast
Most patients don't recall tick bite (nymph ticks are tiny)
Lyme carditis can cause heart block - check ECG
Neurologic Lyme: Bell's palsy, meningitis, radiculopathy
Lyme arthritis is typically monoarticular, large joint (usually knee)
Prevention: tick checks, DEET, permethrin-treated clothing

closureScript.diagnosis: You have Lyme disease. The characteristic 'bull's eye' rash is caused by a bacteria transmitted by deer ticks, almost certainly from your hiking trip. The fact that you have multiple rashes and joint swelling means the infection has spread - this is called 'early disseminated' Lyme.
closureScript.plan: I'm prescribing doxycycline for 21 days - it's longer than the standard course because the infection has disseminated. You should feel better within a few days of starting antibiotics. Your knee swelling should gradually improve.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If you develop new symptoms - especially palpitations, dizziness, facial drooping, or severe headache with stiff neck - come back immediately as these could indicate the infection has spread to your heart or nervous system. Also, complete the full antibiotic course even if you feel better.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: Caught at this stage, Lyme disease is very treatable. For future hikes, I'd recommend checking yourself thoroughly for ticks afterward, using DEET on skin and permethrin on clothes, and showering within 2 hours of coming inside. Your dog should be on tick prevention too.

imaging.result: L4-L5 discitis with adjacent vertebral osteomyelitis. Paravertebral phlegmon. No epidural abscess. No cord compression.
imaging.reasoning: MRI is imaging of choice for vertebral osteomyelitis. Brucella spondylitis typically involves lumbar spine. Must rule out epidural abscess and cord compression.

imaging.name: Transthoracic Echocardiogram
imaging.result: No vegetations. Normal valves. Good LV function.
imaging.reasoning: Brucella endocarditis is serious complication with high mortality. Must screen all brucellosis patients.

imaging.name: TEE (if TTE indeterminate)
imaging.result: Not indicated - TTE clearly negative.
imaging.reasoning: TEE for suspected endocarditis if TTE non-diagnostic. Higher sensitivity for vegetations.

treatments.name: Doxycycline 100mg BID + Gentamicin 5mg/kg/day (FIRST-LINE)
treatments.result: Doxycycline 100mg PO BID started - continue 6 weeks minimum. Gentamicin 5mg/kg IV daily for 2-3 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: DUAL THERAPY is essential - monotherapy has high relapse rate (30-40%). Doxycycline + aminoglycoside is preferred for spondylitis. Gentamicin for 2-3 weeks, then switch to doxy + rifampin.

treatments.name: Doxycycline + Rifampin (alternative/continuation)
treatments.result: After aminoglycoside course, continue doxycycline 100mg BID + rifampin 600-900mg daily for total 6 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: Doxy + rifampin is alternative regimen and used after initial aminoglycoside course. For spondylitis, may need 3-6 months total treatment.

treatments.name: Streptomycin 1g IM daily (alternative aminoglycoside)
treatments.result: Alternative to gentamicin. More historical use.
treatments.reasoning: Streptomycin is traditional but gentamicin equally effective and easier to administer. Either is acceptable.

treatments.name: Spinal surgery (if needed)
treatments.result: Not indicated currently - no epidural abscess, no neurological deficit.
treatments.reasoning: Surgery for: epidural abscess with cord compression, spinal instability, failure of medical therapy. Most spondylitis resolves with antibiotics alone.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: NSAIDs and acetaminophen for back pain. May need short-term opioids for severe pain. Physical therapy once acute phase resolves.
treatments.reasoning: Spondylitis causes significant pain. Multimodal analgesia appropriate.

consults.name: Infectious Disease
consults.result: Confirms brucellosis with spondylitis. Recommends doxycycline + gentamicin x 3 weeks, then doxycycline + rifampin to complete 3-6 months for vertebral involvement.
consults.reasoning: ID expertise essential for prolonged dual-antibiotic therapy and monitoring for relapse.

consults.name: Spine Surgery consult
consults.result: Reviewed MRI - no surgical indication. Will follow if neurological deterioration.
consults.reasoning: Spine surgery involvement helpful for complex spondylitis. Urgent if cord compression develops.

disposition.name: Admit for IV antibiotics
disposition.result: Admitted for IV gentamicin and close monitoring. Can transition to outpatient after aminoglycoside course.
disposition.reasoning: Spondylitis requires IV aminoglycoside initially. May need 2-3 week admission for IV therapy before oral continuation.

disposition.name: Treatment monitoring
disposition.result: Monitor: renal function and aminoglycoside levels (nephrotoxicity), audiometry (ototoxicity), inflammatory markers, serial imaging if symptoms persist.
disposition.reasoning: Aminoglycosides require renal monitoring and drug levels. Check ESR/CRP to track response. Repeat MRI if not improving.

disposition.name: Treatment duration counseling
disposition.result: Spondylitis requires minimum 6 weeks antibiotics, often 3-6 months. Do not stop early even if feeling better - high relapse rate.
disposition.reasoning: Skeletal brucellosis has high relapse rate with short treatment. Prolonged therapy essential. Patient must understand duration.

disposition.name: Public health notification
disposition.result: Brucellosis is reportable. Notified state health department and USDA (animal health).
disposition.reasoning: Brucellosis is reportable disease. USDA will test and potentially quarantine infected herd. Important for eradication program.

disposition.name: Household contact evaluation
disposition.result: Wife also symptomatic - should be evaluated and tested. Family should stop consuming unpasteurized dairy immediately.
disposition.reasoning: Shared exposure through unpasteurized dairy. Other family members at risk.

disposition.name: Prevention counseling
disposition.result: Counseled: No more raw dairy products. Use gloves when assisting calving. Report sick animals to veterinarian.
disposition.reasoning: Prevent reinfection. If cattle are infected, herd needs testing and potentially culling.

disposition.name: Relapse monitoring
disposition.result: Follow-up for 1 year after treatment. Relapse can occur months later. Monitor for recurrent fever, back pain.
disposition.reasoning: Relapse rate 5-15% even with appropriate therapy. Can occur up to 1 year after treatment. Patient needs long-term follow-up.

actualDiagnosis.name: Brucellosis with Vertebral Osteomyelitis (Brucella spondylitis)
teachingPoints: Brucellosis is 'undulant fever' - fever cycles up and down
Transmission: contact with infected animals, unpasteurized dairy
Highest risk: ranchers, vets, slaughterhouse workers
Spondylitis is most common focal complication (especially lumbar)
Also causes orchitis, endocarditis, neurobrucellosis
Diagnosis: blood cultures (prolonged incubation) + serology
Treatment requires DUAL antibiotics for 6+ weeks
Standard: doxycycline + streptomycin or gentamicin

closureScript.diagnosis: You have an infection called brucellosis that you got from your cattle - likely from helping with calving or from drinking raw milk. The bacteria has infected your spine, which is why your back hurts so much.
closureScript.plan: You'll need two antibiotics together for at least 6 weeks, maybe longer because of the spine involvement. We'll monitor you closely with repeat imaging and blood tests.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: This is a serious infection. The spine infection can cause permanent damage if not treated properly. You must take the full course of antibiotics.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: Your wife should be tested too since she's been drinking the same raw milk and has had fevers. Also, I need to report this to public health - they may want to test your cattle herd.

labs.name: Babesia PCR

labs.name: Comprehensive metabolic panel
labs.reasoning: Renal involvement from hemolysis - hemoglobin is nephrotoxic

imaging.name: Chest X-ray

imaging.reasoning: Early ARDS can develop in severe babesiosis

keyFindings: Blood transfusion 2 weeks prior to symptom onset
Ring forms and Maltese cross on peripheral smear
teachingPoints: Babesiosis is the #1 cause of transfusion-transmitted infection fatalities in the US

closureScript.diagnosis: You have an infection called babesiosis that you got from the blood transfusion during your surgery. It's caused by a parasite that infects red blood cells, which is why you're anemic and jaundiced - your body is destroying the infected cells.
closureScript.plan: We're going to admit you and start antibiotics - a combination of azithromycin and atovaquone. We'll monitor your blood counts closely. Because you're on methotrexate, you're at higher risk for complications, so we want to watch you carefully.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: This infection can get worse before it gets better. If your breathing gets worse or you develop confusion, let us know immediately. We'll be checking your blood counts daily.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: This wasn't your fault or the hospital's - this parasite can be in blood donations without the donor knowing. We've notified the blood bank so they can investigate and make sure other recipients are contacted.

male-55-tularemia.oldcartsNarrative: "I went rabbit hunting last weekend. Shot a couple and skinned them myself - done it for years. About 3 days later I noticed a sore on my forearm where I remember cutting myself while dressing one of the rabbits. Didn't think much of it. But then I got a fever - really high, with terrible chills. The sore turned into an ulcer and the glands in my armpit got huge and painful. I've been miserable."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about handling the rabbits
historyQuestions.answer: I shot two rabbits last Saturday. Skinned and dressed them myself in the field. I nicked my forearm with the knife while I was working. Didn't really bleed much.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CLASSIC transmission - handling infected rabbit carcass with skin break

historyQuestions.text: How did the sore on your arm develop?
historyQuestions.answer: Started as a small red bump where I cut myself. Then it became a blister, and now it's an open sore - like a crater. Doesn't really hurt though, which is weird.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic progression: papule → vesicle → painless ulcer

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the armpit swelling
historyQuestions.answer: My left armpit - same side as the sore - got these huge lumps. They're really tender. Started a day or two after the fever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Painful regional lymphadenopathy is KEY feature of ulceroglandular tularemia

historyQuestions.text: When did the fever start?
historyQuestions.answer: About Tuesday or Wednesday - so 3-4 days after the hunting trip. Came on suddenly with terrible chills. Hit 104 at home.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 3-5 day incubation is typical for tularemia

historyQuestions.text: Any tick bites?
historyQuestions.answer: Not that I noticed, but I was in the woods. I checked myself after but didn't find any.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Ticks are another vector for tularemia - important alternative exposure

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

hobbies_hunting.answer: I hunt rabbits and squirrels regularly. Been doing it for 30 years. Always dress them myself in the field.
hobbies_hunting.relevance: RABBIT HUNTING and DRESSING is the exposure. Francisella tularensis is highly infectious - <10 organisms can cause disease.

animal_exposure.answer: Just the rabbits I shot. Also have a dog but he's healthy.
animal_exposure.relevance: Wild rabbits are classic reservoir. Cottontail rabbits in US are frequently infected.

geographic_home_location.answer: I live in rural Arkansas. Hunt on my own property and neighbors'.
geographic_home_location.relevance: South-central US (Arkansas, Missouri, Oklahoma) is endemic for tularemia.

water_exposure_lakes.answer: There's a creek on the property but I don't swim in it.

physicalExam.finding: BP 118/72, HR 96, RR 18, Temp 103.5°F, SpO2 98% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: High fever is hallmark of tularemia - often 104°F+

physicalExam.finding: Left forearm: 1.5cm ulcer with raised, indurated borders and clean base. Minimal surrounding erythema. Not tender to palpation.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic tularemia ulcer: painless, clean, punched-out appearance at inoculation site

physicalExam.finding: Left axillary lymph nodes massively enlarged (4-5cm), tender, warm, fluctuant. Epitrochlear nodes also enlarged on left.
physicalExam.reasoning: Painful regional lymphadenopathy draining the ulcer site = ulceroglandular tularemia

physicalExam.finding: No pharyngeal ulcers or exudates. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out oropharyngeal form (from eating infected meat)

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
physicalExam.reasoning: No pneumonic involvement - important because pneumonic form has higher mortality

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: WBC 14.2 with left shift, mild thrombocytopenia (Plt 128,000)
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with left shift common. Thrombocytopenia seen in tularemia. Can see transaminitis with hepatic involvement.

labs.name: Tularemia serology (tube agglutination)
labs.result: Acute titer 1:80 (suggestive but not diagnostic - need convalescent titer in 2-4 weeks)
labs.reasoning: Serologic diagnosis is standard. Single titer >1:160 is presumptive; 4-fold rise between acute/convalescent is definitive diagnostic.

labs.name: Tularemia PCR (ulcer swab)
labs.reasoning: PCR provides rapid diagnosis from ulcer base swab or lymph node aspirate. Results in 24-48h vs weeks for serology.

labs.name: Blood cultures
labs.result: Pending - LAB NOTIFIED of tularemia suspicion (BSL-3 handling required). Do not routinely culture.
labs.reasoning: Francisella CAN be cultured but requires cysteine-enriched media and BSL-3 biosafety. MUST NOTIFY LAB - lab workers have been infected. Don't delay treatment for culture.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Baseline before treatment. Aminoglycosides are nephrotoxic.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.reasoning: Mild transaminitis common in systemic tularemia. Hepatic involvement can occur.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Clear lung fields, no infiltrates or hilar adenopathy
imaging.reasoning: Rules out pneumonic tularemia - important because pneumonic form has 30-60% mortality if untreated vs <5% for ulceroglandular.

imaging.name: CT Chest (if CXR abnormal)
imaging.result: Not indicated - CXR clear.
imaging.reasoning: CT if CXR shows infiltrates or hilar adenopathy concerning for pneumonic or typhoidal forms.

procedures.name: Lymph node aspiration (if suppurative)
procedures.result: May need aspiration if nodes become fluctuant. Send for PCR/culture.
procedures.reasoning: Suppurative lymph nodes may require aspiration for symptom relief and diagnostic material.

treatments.name: Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID x 14 days (FIRST-LINE)
treatments.result: Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID started. Continue for 14 days.
treatments.reasoning: Fluoroquinolones are first-line for mild-moderate ulceroglandular tularemia. Excellent tissue penetration and oral bioavailability.

treatments.name: Doxycycline 100mg PO BID x 14-21 days (alternative)
treatments.result: Alternative if fluoroquinolone contraindicated. Higher relapse rate than FQ.
treatments.reasoning: Doxycycline is alternative oral option. Higher relapse rate (12-15%) than fluoroquinolones. May need longer course.

treatments.name: Gentamicin 5mg/kg/day IV (for severe disease)
treatments.result: Not indicated for this patient. Reserve for severe/typhoidal tularemia.
treatments.reasoning: Aminoglycosides are most effective but reserved for severe disease due to IV requirement and nephrotoxicity.

treatments.name: Streptomycin 1g IM BID (traditional first-line)
treatments.result: Traditional first-line but less available. Gentamicin is equivalent.
treatments.reasoning: Historical drug of choice but limited availability. Gentamicin equally effective.

treatments.name: AVOID beta-lactams
treatments.result: DO NOT use penicillins, cephalosporins, or carbapenems - Francisella is intrinsically resistant.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Beta-lactams are ineffective against Francisella. Many patients initially treated with amoxicillin before diagnosis - this delays appropriate therapy.

treatments.name: Antipyretics for fever
treatments.result: Acetaminophen 650mg q6h PRN for fever and comfort.
treatments.reasoning: High fevers can be debilitating. Symptomatic management while awaiting antibiotic effect.

consults.name: Infectious Disease consult
consults.result: Confirms suspected tularemia. Agrees with ciprofloxacin. Will coordinate public health reporting.
consults.reasoning: ID expertise valuable for antibiotic selection, treatment duration, and public health coordination.

consults.name: Surgery consult (if nodes suppurate)
consults.result: Not needed currently. Would consult if lymph nodes require I&D.
consults.reasoning: Suppurative nodes occasionally need incision and drainage, though often respond to antibiotics alone.

disposition.name: Hospital admission
disposition.result: Admitted for IV fluids, monitoring, and initiation of treatment. Fever and systemic illness warrant observation.
disposition.reasoning: High fever and systemic illness justify admission. Can consider discharge once afebrile and tolerating oral antibiotics.

disposition.name: Public health reporting
disposition.result: Tularemia is nationally notifiable. Reported to state health department.
disposition.reasoning: Tularemia is reportable disease. Also important for surveillance - unusual clusters could indicate bioterrorism.

disposition.name: Convalescent serology
disposition.result: Repeat serology in 2-4 weeks to confirm diagnosis (4-fold rise is diagnostic).
disposition.reasoning: Paired acute and convalescent titers confirm diagnosis. Important for epidemiologic documentation.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return for: new cough or shortness of breath (pneumonic spread), worsening lymphadenopathy with drainage, persistent fever after 72h of antibiotics.
disposition.reasoning: Pneumonic tularemia development would be serious. Treatment failure requires reassessment.

disposition.name: Prevention counseling
disposition.result: Counseled: Wear gloves when handling game. Don't handle sick-appearing animals. Tick prevention when outdoors. Cook game meat thoroughly.
disposition.reasoning: Prevent reinfection. Gloves provide significant protection when dressing game. Tick prevention also important.

disposition.name: No contact isolation needed
disposition.result: Standard precautions only. Tularemia is NOT person-to-person transmissible.
disposition.reasoning: Reassure patient and family - no risk of transmission to household contacts.

actualDiagnosis.name: Ulceroglandular Tularemia
keyFindings: Rabbit hunting with skin inoculation (knife cut)
teachingPoints: Tularemia = rabbit fever. Classic exposure is skinning/dressing wild rabbits
Ulceroglandular is most common form: painless ulcer + painful lymphadenopathy
Francisella tularensis is highly infectious - lab must be notified (BSL-3)
Beta-lactams DON'T work - need aminoglycosides, doxycycline, or fluoroquinolones
Other exposures: tick bites, handling sick animals, contaminated water, mowing over rabbit carcasses
Pneumonic tularemia (inhaled) has high mortality - ask about respiratory symptoms
Consider bioterrorism if no natural exposure history
Not person-to-person transmissible

closureScript.diagnosis: You have tularemia - also called 'rabbit fever.' You got it from handling that infected rabbit, probably when you cut your finger. The bacteria entered through that small wound and caused the ulcer you see on your arm, and then spread to your lymph nodes.
closureScript.plan: We're starting you on an antibiotic called ciprofloxacin that's effective against this bacteria. Regular antibiotics like penicillin don't work for this one. The fever should start to come down in a day or two, but the lymph nodes may take weeks to shrink.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If you develop a cough or shortness of breath, tell us immediately - that would mean the infection has spread to your lungs. Also, if the lymph nodes in your armpit seem to be getting bigger or you notice pus draining, let us know.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You won't spread this to your family - it's not contagious person to person. In the future, wear gloves when dressing game, and don't handle any animal that seemed sick when you shot it.

actualDiagnosis.name: Vibrio vulnificus Septicemia with Necrotizing Fasciitis
keyFindings: Curved gram-negative rods on blood culture
teachingPoints: Vibrio vulnificus is the DEADLIEST foodborne infection in the US - >50% mortality in septicemia
TWO routes of infection: (1) eating raw shellfish, (2) wound exposure to warm seawater
MAJOR risk factor is LIVER DISEASE (cirrhosis, hepatitis, hemochromatosis) - iron overload feeds Vibrio
Other risk factors: immunosuppression, diabetes, HIV, cancer
HEMORRHAGIC BULLAE are characteristic - blood-filled blisters
Treatment: Doxycycline + third-gen cephalosporin (or fluoroquinolone) AND surgery
Necrotizing fasciitis requires EMERGENT surgical debridement - don't wait for cultures
Gulf Coast warm months (May-October) are highest risk
People with liver disease should NEVER eat raw oysters

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Vibrio vulnificus Prevention and Treatment Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
keyPoints: Individuals with liver disease, cancer, diabetes, HIV, or immunosuppression should avoid raw oysters
Recommended treatment: doxycycline 100mg IV q12h PLUS ceftazidime 2g IV q8h
Alternative: single-agent fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin)
Wound infections require aggressive surgical debridement
Report all V. vulnificus cases to state health department

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Guidelines for Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2014
guidelineReferences.citation: Stevens DL et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10-e52
keyPoints: Necrotizing fasciitis requires emergent surgical exploration and debridement
Do not delay surgery waiting for imaging or culture results
Empiric antibiotics should cover gram-negative organisms in water exposure
Serial debridements often necessary until infection controlled
Mortality increases significantly with each hour of delay in surgery

closureScript.diagnosis: You have a very serious infection called Vibrio vulnificus - you got it from those raw oysters combined with the scrape on your leg. Because of your liver disease, you're extremely vulnerable to this bacteria. It's causing a necrotizing infection - killing the tissue in your leg - and it's spread to your bloodstream.
closureScript.plan: We're taking you to the operating room right now for emergency surgery to remove the infected tissue. This cannot wait. You'll be on powerful antibiotics and in the ICU. I need to be completely honest - this is life-threatening and you may lose part of your leg.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: This is as serious as it gets. Even with everything we're doing, this infection kills more than half the people who get it. But without surgery right now, it's almost always fatal. We're doing everything possible.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is overwhelming. Your family should be here. After this - if you survive, which I'm working hard to make happen - you can never eat raw oysters or shellfish again. With your liver disease, it's simply too dangerous.

female-28-rbf.name: Teresa Nelson
female-28-rbf.chiefComplaint: I have a high fever and this weird rash on my hands and feet. I got bit by a rat at work last week
female-28-rbf.variantDescription: Streptobacillus moniliformis rat-bite fever from pet store exposure

missedConsequences.Rat-Bite Fever: Untreated RBF can cause endocarditis, myocarditis, meningitis, and septic arthritis. Mortality 10-15% if untreated.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about animal bites or exposures in patients with fever and rash
Missing that RBF can occur from rat scratches or handling without bite
Dismissing the rash as viral exanthem
Not recognizing the pattern: bite heals, then systemic illness develops
Using antibiotics that don't cover Streptobacillus (needs penicillin)

allDiagnoses.name: Rat-Bite Fever
allDiagnoses.rationale: Rat bite + fever + migratory polyarthritis + rash on hands/feet = classic RBF.

allDiagnoses.name: Secondary Syphilis

allDiagnoses.rationale: Rash and arthritis pattern, but bite history points elsewhere.

actualDiagnosis.name: Rat-Bite Fever (Streptobacillus moniliformis)
keyFindings: Rat bite 10 days prior (bite wound healed)
Sudden onset high fever after incubation period
Maculopapular and petechial rash on palms and soles
teachingPoints: Rat-bite fever = relapsing fever + migratory polyarthritis + rash after rat bite/exposure
Caused by Streptobacillus moniliformis (North America) or Spirillum minus (Asia)
Bite wound typically HEALS before systemic illness - don't be falsely reassured
Can also occur from scratches, handling rats, or consuming rat-contaminated food (Haverhill fever)
Rash on palms/soles - differential includes secondary syphilis, RMSF, endocarditis
Treatment: Penicillin G is drug of choice. Doxycycline if allergic.
Untreated mortality 10-15%, but rarely fatal with appropriate antibiotics
Complications: endocarditis, myocarditis, meningitis, abscess formation

closureScript.diagnosis: You have rat-bite fever - an infection you got from that rat bite at work. The bacteria from the rat's mouth got into your bloodstream and is causing the fever, the joint swelling, and the rash. It's classic that the bite itself healed but then you got sick a week later.
closureScript.plan: We're admitting you for IV penicillin - that's the best antibiotic for this infection. You'll need about two weeks of treatment total. We also did an echocardiogram to make sure the bacteria didn't infect your heart valves - that can be a complication - and fortunately it looks okay.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: With antibiotics, you should start feeling better in a few days. The joint pain may take a bit longer to resolve. If you develop chest pain, shortness of breath, or worsening symptoms, tell us right away.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You'll need to report this as a workplace injury for workers' compensation. Also, your employer should know about proper handling techniques and consider gloves when handling rats. This is a known risk in pet stores, but can be prevented with precautions.

actualDiagnosis.name: Dengue Fever with Warning Signs
keyFindings: Recent travel to Puerto Rico (dengue endemic area)
High fever with severe myalgias/arthralgias ('breakbone fever')
Classic rash with 'islands of white in sea of red'
Warning signs: abdominal pain, vomiting, hepatomegaly, pleural effusion
teachingPoints: Dengue is the most common arboviral infection globally - 100-400 million cases/year
Transmitted by Aedes aegypti (and A. albopictus) - bite during DAYTIME
Classic features: high fever, severe headache (retro-orbital), severe myalgias/arthralgias, rash
WARNING SIGNS (predict severe dengue): abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, liver enlargement, rising HCT with falling platelets
CRITICAL PHASE occurs when fever breaks (day 3-7) - paradoxically when plasma leak is worst
DO NOT give aspirin or NSAIDs - increases bleeding risk
Fluid management is critical: too much → pulmonary edema; too little → shock
Secondary infection (different serotype) carries higher risk of severe dengue

closureScript.diagnosis: You have dengue fever - you got it from mosquito bites in Puerto Rico. It's sometimes called 'breakbone fever' because of the severe pain, which you're definitely experiencing. Your blood tests confirm the infection.
closureScript.plan: We're admitting you because you have some warning signs - the stomach pain, vomiting, and some fluid shifts we're seeing. The critical time is when your fever breaks in the next day or two - that's paradoxically when things can get worse. We need to monitor you closely and manage your fluids very carefully.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Don't take any aspirin or ibuprofen - those can cause bleeding problems with dengue. If you notice any bleeding - from your gums, in your stool, or heavy menstrual bleeding - tell us immediately. Also let us know if you feel dizzy or your stomach pain gets worse.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: Most people with dengue recover completely, but because you have warning signs, we want to watch you carefully. The next few days are important. For future travel, use DEET repellent and cover up during daytime when these mosquitoes bite.
